unit.name: Compounding Vessel (Jacketed Mixing Tank)
unit.cfj: Dissolve and blend active and excipient components into homogeneous bulk solution

meta.method: Ulwick ODI (Importance × Satisfaction → Opportunity), needs scoring v2

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of active ingredient concentration variation among vessel locations after mixing
rows.plain: Make sure the active ingredient is spread evenly throughout the vessel after mixing. This matters so that each part of the product has the intended strength.
rows.imp_rat: Active concentration variation among vessel locations directly measures blend uniformity; severe patient-dose consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Standard agitation designs achieve positional uniformity in most tanks, though geometry-driven low-flow pockets still cause occasional variation. Location-to-location uniformity is confirmed by sampling several points, adding wait time before proceeding. Identifying dead zones and interpreting positional variance requires seasoned process knowledge. Positional non-uniformity can scrap batches or cause underdosed product, driving material waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of active and excipient concentration variation across the target-volume batch
rows.plain: Keep the amounts of the medicine and its other ingredients consistent throughout a batch, even when the batch size changes.
rows.imp_rat: Concentration variation across the batch is the essence of the homogeneity job; high severity as it drives dose-uniformity failures.
rows.sat_rat: Well-designed impellers and validated mix times deliver homogeneity in most batches, but stratification in large tanks remains an intermittent failure. Confirming uniform concentration across the batch requires multi-point sampling that delays release. Interpreting whether the whole batch is uniform demands analytical and process expertise. Concentration variation risks rejecting the entire batch, a large material and energy loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of undissolved active or excipient material remaining when target-volume mixing ends
rows.plain: Make sure no undissolved medicine or other ingredient remains when mixing the planned amount is finished.
rows.imp_rat: Undissolved material at mixing end directly defeats the CFJ of dissolving components into homogeneous solution; residual undissolved active means out-of-spec potency, a serious patient-safety/regulatory failure.
rows.sat_rat: Validated recipes and endpoint checks catch most cases, but residual undissolved fines still slip through often enough to remain a recurring concern. Reaching a verified fully-dissolved endpoint still depends on visual checks and hold times that add unpredictable minutes to hours before completion is confirmed. Operators lean on experienced judgement to decide when the last solids have dissolved, since routine in-line dissolution sensing is rarely deployed. Undissolved material at target volume forces rework, filtration or batch loss, wasting expensive active and excipient.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of undissolved active ingredient remaining at the specified mixing endpoint
rows.plain: Leave as little undissolved medicine as possible when mixing is supposed to be complete. This helps make sure the mixture has the intended amount of medicine throughout.
rows.imp_rat: Undissolved active at endpoint is core to dissolution job; residual active undersolubilizes potency, a severe content-uniformity failure.
rows.sat_rat: Established mixing profiles reliably dissolve most actives, but poorly wetting or agglomerating actives still occasionally leave residue. Confirming the active is fully dissolved at the endpoint adds sampling and analysis delay that extends the batch. Detecting trace undissolved active depends on skilled visual inspection or off-line assay rather than an automated indicator. Any undissolved active is costly product loss given the high value of the active fraction.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of concentration nonuniformity across the batch at the required endpoint
rows.plain: Make sure the batch has the same concentration throughout when the process is finished. This matters so that different portions of the batch are not weaker or stronger than intended.
rows.imp_rat: Concentration nonuniformity at the required endpoint is the essence of the homogeneity job; severe dose-uniformity failure.
rows.sat_rat: Concentration uniformity is generally achievable with proper mixing but gradients recur in poorly mixed geometries. Multi-point concentration checks add sampling and assay time. Interpreting uniformity data requires standard analytical competence. Nonuniform batches risk rejection and reprocessing cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of active and excipient concentration variation within batches operated inside the proposed ranges
rows.plain: Reduce how much the amounts of the main ingredients and added materials vary from one part of a batch to another when operating within the proposed settings. This matters so the product is made consistently.
rows.imp_rat: Concentration variation within batches operated inside ranges is central to blend uniformity; severe potency-distribution consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Validated ranges generally hold variation acceptable, but not every in-range corner is proven, leaving residual risk. Confirming low variation across the full operating range needs repeated sampling that adds time. Assessing variation robustness inside ranges requires statistical and formulation expertise. Excess variation in-range risks batch rejection and material loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that system KPI status indicates acceptable control while blend homogeneity or GMP control is deteriorating
rows.plain: Make it less likely that general system indicators say everything is under control while the mixture is becoming uneven or quality controls are weakening. This matters because a problem could be missed until the product is affected.
rows.imp_rat: KPI indicating acceptable control while homogeneity/GMP deteriorates masks a failing batch; relevant with very high severity as an undetected escape.
rows.sat_rat: KPIs frequently reflect lagging or aggregated indicators that can show acceptable status while underlying homogeneity control drifts, a well-recognized and poorly solved gap. KPI dashboards update on reporting cycles that can lag real deterioration in blend control. Interpreting whether green KPIs mask true drift requires seasoned quality judgment that is scarce. KPI/monitoring systems are already funded, so cost is not the barrier.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of concentration variation across vessel locations at the homogeneity decision
rows.plain: At the time the mixing result is judged, the amount of ingredient should be nearly the same throughout the container. This helps show that the whole mixture is even.
rows.imp_rat: Concentration variation at the homogeneity decision point is core to CFJ; severe because a nonuniform batch can be wrongly released.
rows.sat_rat: Established multi-location sampling protocols reliably characterize spatial uniformity in validated vessels, though dead zones can still be missed. Multi-point sampling adds handling time but is scheduled into the batch, limiting delay. Interpreting spatial concentration variance requires analytical support and some judgement on sampling locations. Detecting and correcting spatial variation avoids costly rejects but multi-point testing consumes labor and assay resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of component concentration variation across the batch during mixing and hold
rows.plain: The amount of each ingredient should stay as even as possible throughout the group of products while it is mixed and held. This helps ensure the products have the intended strength.
rows.imp_rat: Concentration variation across the batch during mixing and hold is core to CFJ; severe because non-uniformity carries to fill.
rows.sat_rat: Continued agitation during hold usually maintains uniformity, though settling or stratification during static holds remains a recurring gap. Monitoring concentration through mixing and hold adds sampling delays to the process. Judging when transient variation has settled during hold takes experienced interpretation. Variation during hold can force remixing, wasting energy and time-linked cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a candidate vessel cannot achieve blend homogeneity across specified fill volumes and fluid properties
rows.plain: Avoid choosing a vessel that cannot mix the contents evenly for the required amounts and types of liquid. Even mixing helps ensure each filled unit has the right contents.
rows.imp_rat: A vessel that cannot achieve homogeneity across fill volumes fails the core job outright; severe because unfit equipment jeopardizes every batch.
rows.sat_rat: Standard blend-time correlations and vendor sizing handle common cases, but non-Newtonian or wide fill-range fluids still spring surprises at spec time. Mixing studies and CFD/scale-down trials take time to run but are established steps in the specification cycle. Predicting homogeneity across varied fluid properties still leans heavily on scarce mixing-engineering judgement and vendor expertise. Trial batches and pilot studies to confirm homogeneity consume material and engineering resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of required component mass left undissolved within permitted batch constraints
rows.plain: Reduce how much of an ingredient remains unmixed or undissolved in the batch. This helps ensure the finished batch contains the intended amount throughout.
rows.imp_rat: Undissolved mass within permitted constraints is core dissolution shortfall; severe potency consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Achieving full dissolution within constrained batch windows is usually attainable but not guaranteed for difficult components. Verifying dissolution within batch time constraints adds bounded effort. Balancing dissolution completeness against batch limits needs process judgement. Undissolved material within constraints means lost yield.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of batches containing undissolved active or excipient material when operated within the proposed ranges
rows.plain: Make sure fewer production batches contain solid ingredients that have not dissolved, when the process is run within the planned settings. This helps ensure each batch is made evenly and works as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Batches with undissolved material within proposed ranges strike at the core dissolution job; each such batch is a serious potency failure.
rows.sat_rat: Validated ranges usually keep failures rare, but edge combinations within range still occasionally yield undissolved solids. Discovering undissolved material within approved ranges typically surfaces late, extending investigation time. Setting robust ranges that avoid failures requires substantial development expertise. Batches with undissolved material within nominally valid ranges are direct scrap or rework losses.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of component mass left undissolved at the required batch endpoint
rows.plain: Leave as little of the ingredient undissolved as possible when the batch is supposed to be finished. This helps ensure the mixture has the intended makeup.
rows.imp_rat: Component mass left undissolved at the required endpoint is a direct CFJ failure; severe potency/uniformity impact.
rows.sat_rat: Standard endpoint checks work well for typical formulations but miss slow-dissolving edge cases. Endpoint sampling and analysis add manageable time to batch confirmation. Confirming endpoint dissolution uses routine but attention-dependent technique. Residual undissolved mass represents wasted active material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of required component mass undissolved at the defined blend endpoint
rows.plain: Leave as little of the required ingredient undissolved as possible when mixing is complete. This helps ensure the finished mixture is evenly made throughout.
rows.imp_rat: Required component mass undissolved at blend endpoint is a direct dissolution failure and potency shortfall; core with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Dissolution endpoint verification is fairly mature, though sampling limitations leave occasional undetected undissolved fractions. Dissolution endpoint studies add defined but bounded time. Judging complete dissolution requires moderate analytical skill and sampling technique. Undissolved mass wastes active and can fail the batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the bulk solution departs from its homogeneity specification before fill completion
rows.plain: Keep the bulk mixture evenly mixed until filling is finished. This helps ensure that each filled container receives the intended mixture.
rows.imp_rat: Loss of homogeneity before fill completion means non-uniform dose delivery downstream, central to the blend job; severity is high because it produces out-of-spec product.
rows.sat_rat: Validated mixing parameters and in-process sampling make sustained homogeneity common, but sampling gaps and settling of some formulations leave residual excursions. In-process homogeneity checks and validated mix times keep the solution within spec through most of the fill window, though checks occur at intervals rather than continuously. Validated recipes reduce reliance on operator judgement, but detecting slow drift before fill completion still depends on experienced oversight. Occasional out-of-spec drift forces reblend or rejection, imposing material and time costs on the batch.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of nonhomogeneous batches approved as conforming
rows.plain: Prevent batches that are not evenly mixed from being approved as acceptable. This helps ensure that every part of the batch has the intended composition.
rows.imp_rat: Nonhomogeneous batches approved as conforming is a direct escape of defective product; severity is very high as patients receive out-of-spec doses.
rows.sat_rat: Defined acceptance criteria and dual review make erroneous conforming approvals uncommon, though sampling bias can still let borderline batches through. Approval decisions wait on assay results, adding some lag but not chronic delay. Disposition depends on QA judgement and correct interpretation of homogeneity data, a skill-sensitive step. A nonhomogeneous batch released as conforming risks costly recall and rework, a significant downside.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of in-range parameter combinations that yield unacceptable dissolution, homogeneity, or stability
rows.plain: Avoid operating settings that appear acceptable but still produce products that do not dissolve properly, are not evenly mixed, or do not remain stable. This helps ensure the product works as intended and stays usable.
rows.imp_rat: In-range parameter combinations yielding unacceptable results undermine the validated operating space; severe because they cause hidden batch failures.
rows.sat_rat: Ranges are typically validated at set points rather than exhaustively, so unacceptable in-range combinations can persist undetected. Finding which in-range combinations fail requires extended DOE studies that take significant development time. Mapping the failure envelope inside approved ranges is a high-expertise development activity. Undetected bad combinations cause failed batches that waste materials.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the recommended vessel fails dissolution or blending criteria at intended minimum or maximum batch volumes
rows.plain: Choose a mixing container that works properly both when it is filled as little as intended and when it is filled as much as intended. The contents should dissolve and mix well at both amounts.
rows.imp_rat: Vessel failing dissolution/blend at min or max batch volume means it is unfit at the range extremes; central with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Min/max volume qualification is standard intent but bracketing is often incomplete, so failures at extremes still occur. Testing at both volume extremes lengthens PQ campaigns considerably. Predicting min/max volume performance requires mixing and scale-up expertise that is scarce. Full-range batch testing consumes significant material and vessel time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing blend homogeneity at intended fill-volume or viscosity extremes
rows.plain: Lower the chance that a mixture becomes uneven when the amount being filled or the mixture’s thickness is at either end of the intended range. This helps ensure each filled unit is made the same way.
rows.imp_rat: Losing homogeneity at fill-volume/viscosity extremes is a core blend-robustness failure; relevant with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Extremes of fill and viscosity are the conditions most likely to break homogeneity and are inconsistently covered. Testing homogeneity at fill and viscosity extremes lengthens evaluation. Anticipating extreme-condition mixing behavior requires specialized knowledge. Lost homogeneity at extremes drives batch waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of component precipitation during planned vessel holds or thermal transitions
rows.plain: Prevent ingredients from separating out or forming unwanted solid material while they are held in a container or moved between temperatures. This helps keep the product evenly mixed and consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Precipitation during holds or thermal transitions destroys homogeneity and potency; relevant with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Precipitation during holds and thermal transitions is a real risk imperfectly predicted from solubility data. Evaluating hold-stability adds thermal-cycle study time. Predicting precipitation risk requires solubility and formulation expertise. Precipitation ruins batches, causing significant loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of undissolved active ingredient remaining after completion of the addition sequence
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of the active medicine that remains undissolved after all additions are finished. This helps ensure the mixture is properly prepared before the next step.
rows.imp_rat: Undissolved active after addition sequence is central to dissolution; severe because affected batch fails potency specs.
rows.sat_rat: Sequenced addition with validated agitation dissolves most material, yet late-added or clumped active can persist. Verifying no undissolved active remains after the addition sequence requires a hold and check that consumes batch time. Judging completeness after charging relies on operator experience with the specific formulation. Undissolved active after addition means reprocessing costs and possible loss of high-value material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of closing the disruption while the bulk solution remains nonhomogeneous
rows.plain: Lower the chance that a production problem is declared resolved while the main mixture is still uneven. This helps ensure the product is properly mixed before work continues.
rows.imp_rat: Closing the process while still nonhomogeneous releases a defective batch; central to job completion with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Requirement for confirming results before closure makes premature closure uncommon, though pressure to proceed occasionally overrides verification. Confirmatory sampling before closing a disruption adds verification time but is procedurally expected. Judging whether homogeneity is truly restored requires competent interpretation of confirming data. Prematurely closing on a nonhomogeneous batch risks downstream reject costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of composition variation between vessel sampling locations at the blend endpoint
rows.plain: Make sure samples taken from different places in the vessel have nearly the same mixture at the end of blending. This shows that the product is mixed evenly throughout.
rows.imp_rat: Composition variation between sampling locations at blend endpoint is core homogeneity failure; severe uniformity consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Spatial composition uniformity is reasonably managed with good mixing but sampling coverage gaps leave residual uncertainty. Multi-location sampling at endpoint adds test time. Correct sampling location selection depends on mixing knowledge. Location-to-location variation risks costly nonconformance.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of intended batch conditions in which blend uniformity fails acceptance criteria
rows.plain: Reduce the chances that the ingredients are unevenly mixed during normal production. An even mixture helps ensure each part of the batch has the intended strength.
rows.imp_rat: Intended conditions where blend uniformity fails acceptance is a direct CFJ failure across the operating range; relevant with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Blend uniformity acceptance testing is a mature practice, so failures within intended conditions are relatively uncommon. Blend-uniformity sampling and assay across conditions extends qualification timelines. Designing valid sampling plans for uniformity needs experienced formulation and statistics staff. Extensive sampling and assay drive analytical cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that homogeneity demonstrated at evaluation scale fails at production scale
rows.plain: Make sure a mixture that is uniform in small-scale testing stays uniform when made in full production. This helps ensure every part of the product has the intended contents.
rows.imp_rat: Homogeneity passing at eval scale but failing at production is a scale-up risk that undermines every commercial batch; relevant with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Bench-to-production homogeneity translation remains a classic failure mode that standard practice only partially mitigates. Scale-up confirmation trials add significant time before capability is trusted. Reliable scale-up prediction is a specialized mixing science few practitioners command well. Homogeneity failures at production scale trigger expensive batch losses and rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of active-ingredient concentration variation among vessel locations at the blend endpoint
rows.plain: Make the active ingredient equally concentrated throughout the mixing container at the end of mixing. This helps ensure each portion has the intended amount of medicine.
rows.imp_rat: Active-concentration variation among locations at blend endpoint is core homogeneity failure; severe dose-uniformity consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Validated mixing plus endpoint sampling makes uniform solutions reliably achievable for most formulations. Confirming uniformity requires blend time and sampling turnaround. Judging homogeneity depends on validated parameters more than moment-to-moment skill. Concentration variation risks batch rejection, a high-value loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that candidate capability conclusions fail at intended batch scale
rows.plain: Make sure conclusions about what a possible solution can do still hold when it is used at the size of a real production batch. This helps avoid choosing something that works only in small tests.
rows.imp_rat: Capability conclusions failing at intended scale means the selected vessel underperforms in production; relevant with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Conclusions drawn at evaluation scale often fail to hold at batch scale, a persistent and imperfectly managed risk. Validating conclusions at intended batch scale prolongs qualification. Extrapolating capability conclusions to full scale requires scarce scale-up judgement. Wrong scale conclusions mean costly late-stage equipment changes.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percent of batch material remaining undissolved at homogeneity assessment
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of material left undissolved when the mixture is checked for evenness. This helps ensure that the batch is properly dissolved and mixed.
rows.imp_rat: Percent undissolved at homogeneity assessment is a direct measure of CFJ success; consequences are serious potency/uniformity nonconformance.
rows.sat_rat: Validated dissolution parameters make full dissolution the norm for well-characterized formulations, with residual failures on difficult actives. Dissolution endpoints are checked at set times, so undissolved material is usually caught before proceeding but not instantly. Visual and analytical checks for undissolved solids are routine and require moderate operator competence. Undissolved fractions trigger extended mixing or rejection, wasting material and energy.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of microbial, particulate, or cross-product contamination during dissolution and blending
rows.plain: Prevent germs, unwanted particles, or another product from getting into the medicine while it is being dissolved and mixed. This helps keep the product safe and consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Microbial/particulate/cross-product contamination during compounding is catastrophic for a pharmaceutical bulk solution, though tangential to the dissolve-and-blend mechanics; consequences are severe patient harm.
rows.sat_rat: Mature cleaning validation, closed vessels, and environmental monitoring substantially reduce contamination, though residual excursions still occur and are hard to eliminate. Environmental and bioburden monitoring results lag the blending step, so contamination is often confirmed after the fact rather than prevented in real time. Aseptic technique, cleaning validation, and contamination-control expertise are demanding and depend heavily on well-trained personnel. Cleanroom controls, cleaning validation, and monitoring are expensive but treated as non-negotiable GMP infrastructure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of active or excipient material settled in lower vessel regions during the pre-fill hold
rows.plain: Keep the active ingredient and other ingredients from collecting at the bottom of the container while the mixture waits to be filled. This helps ensure every filled product has the right mixture.
rows.imp_rat: Material settling during pre-fill hold destroys the achieved homogeneity before fill; relevant with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Minimizing pre-fill hold and agitation practice limits settling, but it still occurs during interruptions. Re-suspending settled material before mixing adds handling time. Managing pre-fill holds to avoid settling takes operational awareness. Settled material can be lost or cause off-spec, wasting product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of active or excipient material degraded by mixing exposure at the target volume
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of medicine ingredient or other added material damaged by mixing at the intended batch size. This helps preserve the product’s quality and effectiveness.
rows.imp_rat: Material degraded by mixing exposure reduces potency and creates impurities; strongly relevant with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Validated conditions usually limit degradation, but scale-up thermal and shear effects occasionally push it up. Quantifying degradation at target volume needs analytical turnaround that adds time. Predicting scale-dependent degradation requires specialized process knowledge. Material degraded at scale is costly lost product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the corrective response fails to restore dissolution and blend homogeneity
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that the fix for a production problem fails to fully dissolve the material and mix it evenly. This helps restore the mixture to the required condition.
rows.imp_rat: A corrective response that fails to restore homogeneity leaves a defective batch unrecoverable; central to the job's robustness with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Standard reblend procedures usually restore homogeneity, but success is not guaranteed for degraded or heavily deviating batches. Corrective reblending consumes additional mixing cycles that extend the batch timeline. Choosing an effective corrective response leans heavily on experienced operators and process knowledge. A failed corrective attempt wastes further material, energy and time before the batch is salvaged or lost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that collected samples do not represent component distribution throughout the compounding vessel
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that collected samples fail to show how ingredients are spread throughout the mixing container. This helps confirm that the whole mixture is evenly mixed.
rows.imp_rat: Non-representative samples misjudge distribution, allowing nonuniform batches to pass; relevant with high severity as an escape risk.
rows.sat_rat: Sampling that fails to capture true spatial distribution in a large tank is a well-known blind spot, and validation of sampling representativeness is often weaker than assay reliability itself. Representative sampling plans are executed within the normal batch cycle, so timing is not the primary concern. Designing and executing spatially representative sampling of a large vessel demands real sampling expertise and is error-prone. Multiple sampling points and analyses increase cost modestly but acceptably.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of vessel working volume outside composition acceptance limits under worst-case intended conditions
rows.plain: Keep more of the mixture within the required ingredient ranges throughout the container, even under the most difficult expected conditions. This helps ensure the product is evenly mixed and has the right composition.
rows.imp_rat: Working volume outside composition limits under worst case is direct non-uniformity; relevant to CFJ with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Dead zones and stratification under worst-case conditions are inconsistently predicted, leaving real recurring gaps. Assessing worst-case composition uniformity across working volume takes added sampling time. Identifying worst-case volume conditions needs experienced process interpretation. Out-of-spec volume fractions cause batch rejection costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of active or excipient degradation produced at the proposed operating boundaries
rows.plain: Limit damage to the medicine ingredients and other ingredients when choosing the equipment settings. This helps keep the product effective and consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Degradation at operating boundaries threatens product quality across the range; relevant with high severity for impurity/potency impacts.
rows.sat_rat: Boundaries are often less thoroughly characterized for degradation, leaving meaningful residual uncertainty. Assessing degradation at operating boundaries requires additional stability testing time. Understanding degradation at range edges is a demanding development task. Boundary degradation wastes active material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of charging the wrong component because its identity is ambiguous in the addition sequence
rows.plain: Make the order for adding ingredients clear enough that workers do not add the wrong one. This helps prevent mistakes in the product.
rows.imp_rat: Charging the wrong component from ambiguous identity is a catastrophic wrong-material error; lower mixing-mechanics relevance but severe patient-safety consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Barcoding and dispensing checks reduce mix-ups, but ambiguous labeling still leaves residual human-error risk. Resolving ambiguous component identity during charging can stall the sequence. Avoiding wrong-component charges depends heavily on operator attention and clear labeling rather than automated verification. A wrong component ruins the batch, a large material loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of active ingredient degradation caused by the established mixing conditions
rows.plain: Keep the active medicine from breaking down because of how it is mixed. This helps the finished product remain effective.
rows.imp_rat: Active degradation from mixing conditions defeats the goal of a usable homogeneous solution; severe potency loss and impurity generation.
rows.sat_rat: Validated gentle mixing conditions keep degradation low in most batches, with rare excursions. Confirming degradation stays low requires stability assays that delay disposition. Setting mixing conditions that avoid degradation demands formulation and thermal expertise. Degraded active is unrecoverable product loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that mixing requirements omit production-scale blending behavior
rows.plain: Make sure the mixing instructions reflect how the process behaves when it is run at full production size. This helps prevent a mix from turning out differently when production increases.
rows.imp_rat: Requirements omitting production-scale blending behavior risk scale-up failure; relevant upstream with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Requirements frequently reflect bench behavior and omit production-scale mixing dynamics, a recurring source of downstream failure. Adding production-scale blending analysis extends the evaluation timeline. Anticipating full-scale blending behavior from lab data is a scarce scale-up specialty. Ignoring scale effects risks costly capacity or capability shortfalls after purchase.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of component precipitation caused by an incompatible addition order in the compounding vessel
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that an ingredient forms unwanted solid particles because ingredients were added in the wrong order. The correct order helps keep the mixture usable and consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Precipitation from incompatible addition order directly destroys homogeneity and dissolution; severe because it can ruin the whole batch.
rows.sat_rat: Validated addition sequences prevent precipitation reliably when followed, so this failure is uncommon in mature processes. Precipitation from bad addition order forces investigation and reprocessing that consume batch time. Preventing incompatibility precipitation relies heavily on formulator knowledge of addition sequence. A precipitation event typically scraps the batch, a major material loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of persistent low-circulation regions within the compounding vessel
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that parts of the mixing container have very little movement. Good movement throughout the container helps keep the mixture evenly blended.
rows.imp_rat: Persistent low-circulation dead zones cause localized non-uniformity, central to blend job; severe as they yield undetected out-of-spec regions.
rows.sat_rat: Baffles and validated impeller placement reduce dead zones, but tank geometry still leaves occasional stubborn low-flow regions. Low-circulation zones prolong dissolution and homogenization, extending mix time. Recognizing and mitigating dead zones requires mixing-engineering expertise not always on hand. Persistent dead zones cause off-spec batches and wasted material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that homogeneity samples omit concentration extremes within the vessel
rows.plain: Take samples from enough different places in the vessel to include areas with both lower and higher concentrations. This helps show whether the contents are mixed evenly.
rows.imp_rat: Samples that omit concentration extremes can miss non-uniformity, letting defective batches pass; relevant with high severity as an undetected escape.
rows.sat_rat: Validated sampling plans target representative and worst-case locations, but manual sampling can still systematically miss true extremes. Following a defined sampling plan takes fixed time and does not add much delay. Ensuring samples capture concentration extremes depends heavily on the sampling-plan design skill and disciplined execution. Missing extremes leads to undetected defects with high downstream cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of the intended fill-volume, viscosity, thermal, and batch-size envelope absent from capability evaluation
rows.plain: Test the process across as much of the planned range of fill amount, thickness, temperature, and batch size as possible. This shows whether the process can work reliably under different expected conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Envelope gaps in capability evaluation risk deploying an unfit vessel; relevant upstream with high severity across all batches.
rows.sat_rat: Evaluations typically cover nominal and a few extremes, leaving portions of the operating envelope untested and prone to failure. Mapping the full envelope of fill, viscosity, thermal and batch-size cases is time-consuming and often truncated. Defining the worst-case corners of a multi-variable envelope takes experienced process judgement. Unevaluated envelope regions become costly capability gaps discovered in production.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in formulation homogeneity endpoints and acceptance criteria
rows.plain: Make the expected point for an evenly mixed product and the rules for accepting it clearer. Clear requirements help people know when mixing is complete and acceptable.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous homogeneity endpoints and acceptance criteria undermine consistent release decisions; relevant with high severity for compliance.
rows.sat_rat: Homogeneity endpoints and acceptance criteria are frequently vague at specification time, a recurring source of error. Clarifying homogeneity criteria upfront saves later rework time but is often skipped. Defining unambiguous acceptance criteria requires cross-functional formulation and QA expertise. Ambiguous criteria cause costly disputes and re-testing.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a critical vessel KPI exceeds its approved threshold without detection before bulk-solution transfer
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that an important vessel measurement goes above its allowed limit without anyone noticing before the solution is moved out. Early warning helps prevent an unsuitable batch from being transferred.
rows.imp_rat: An undetected KPI breach before transfer allows a defective batch to proceed; relevant to control with high safety severity.
rows.sat_rat: Alarms and pre-transfer checks catch most excursions, but manual or gapped monitoring leaves detection windows. KPI review before transfer is often periodic, so a threshold breach may persist briefly before it is caught. Interpreting whether a KPI truly exceeds an approved limit requires trained oversight, especially with manual logging. An undetected excursion transferred downstream risks costly batch loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that evaluation sampling misses localized composition gradients within the vessel
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that samples miss areas where the solution is unevenly mixed. This helps confirm that the whole vessel has the expected composition.
rows.imp_rat: Evaluation sampling missing localized gradients can validate an inhomogeneous process; relevant with high severity as a systemic escape.
rows.sat_rat: Sampling plans routinely miss localized composition pockets, a persistent detection gap. Comprehensive gradient sampling adds substantial test time often shortcut. Designing sampling that catches localized gradients requires specialized statistical and mixing knowledge. Missed gradients cause escaped nonconformances and recalls.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that material properties used to specify mixing capability are inaccurate across expected operating conditions
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that incorrect information about the solution is used when deciding how well it must be mixed. This helps ensure the mixing equipment is suitable under all expected conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Inaccurate material properties used to specify capability can cause systemic mis-sizing; relevant upstream with high severity if unfit vessel is chosen.
rows.sat_rat: Property data is often taken at nominal conditions and viscosity/density shifts with temperature and concentration routinely undermine specification accuracy. Verifying material property data across conditions delays finalizing the spec. Judging whether literature or single-point property data extrapolates correctly requires specialized material knowledge. Inaccurate properties lead to mis-sized equipment and expensive corrections.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that anticipated batch scales, solution properties, or operating extremes fall outside qualification coverage
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that testing does not cover the expected batch sizes, solution types, or unusual operating conditions. This helps show that the process will work safely in the situations where it is expected to run.
rows.imp_rat: Operating extremes falling outside qualification coverage leaves unvalidated conditions in use; relevant upstream with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Qualification coverage often reflects current known conditions, so future scales or extremes routinely fall outside it. Anticipating full operating ranges early is time-consuming when future batch scales are uncertain. Forecasting operating extremes and solution properties demands deep process knowledge that few possess. Under-scoped coverage forces costly re-qualification when ranges are exceeded.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of charged active and excipient quantities deviating from batch targets
rows.plain: The amounts of active ingredients and other added materials should stay closer to the amounts planned for each batch. This helps keep the finished product consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Charged quantities deviating from targets directly corrupt formulation composition; central to correct concentration with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Weigh-and-verify procedures and dispensing controls make charging deviations uncommon in mature operations. Verifying charged quantities against targets adds weigh-check steps. Accurate charging depends on operator diligence and dispensing skill. Charging errors waste material and can scrap batches.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts between mixing requirements and sterile-boundary or contamination-control constraints
rows.plain: Fewer unresolved disagreements should remain between mixing needs and the rules for keeping products and clean areas protected from contamination. Resolving these issues helps the mixing process work without compromising cleanliness.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved conflicts with sterile-boundary/contamination constraints can undermine sterility; moderate mixing relevance but high contamination severity.
rows.sat_rat: Conflicts between mixing requirements and sterile-boundary constraints are commonly unresolved until late. Reconciling mixing needs with sterile/contamination constraints adds coordination time. Resolving these conflicts needs combined sterile-process and mixing expertise. Unresolved conflicts drive costly redesign or contamination events.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of heat-transfer fluid entering the batch through a compromised jacket boundary during mixing
rows.plain: Prevent the liquid used to control temperature from leaking through a damaged outer wall into the product during mixing. This protects the product from being contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Heat-transfer fluid entering the batch through a compromised jacket is direct product contamination/adulteration; moderate mixing relevance but catastrophic severity.
rows.sat_rat: Jacket integrity testing and pressure monitoring make breaches rare, though not impossible. A jacket breach forces immediate stop and investigation, a major time loss. Detecting fluid ingress requires monitoring and maintenance expertise. Heat-transfer fluid contamination scraps the batch, a severe loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that specified agitation conditions damage shear-sensitive formulation components
rows.plain: Make sure the required mixing does not harm ingredients that can be damaged by strong movement. This protects the quality of the finished mixture.
rows.imp_rat: Agitation damaging shear-sensitive components degrades product quality; relevant with high severity for such formulations.
rows.sat_rat: Shear-sensitivity limits are often poorly characterized at spec time, so damaging conditions can be specified unknowingly. Determining safe shear windows for sensitive components adds study time. Predicting shear damage thresholds requires specialized formulation-sensitivity expertise. Shear damage degrades expensive actives, driving batch loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of component degradation caused by candidate agitation across specified operating conditions
rows.plain: Limit damage to parts caused by the movement used by the proposed equipment under the required operating conditions. This helps show whether the equipment can handle the parts safely.
rows.imp_rat: Component degradation from candidate agitation across conditions affects vessel fitness and quality; relevant with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Degradation across the full range of agitation conditions is inconsistently characterized, leaving residual risk. Characterizing degradation across the operating range extends testing. Assessing cumulative shear degradation demands specialized analytical judgement. Degradation wastes active material and batches.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of shear-sensitive component degradation caused by agitation during compounding
rows.plain: Reduce damage to components that can be harmed by strong mixing during preparation. This helps the process work properly and protects product quality.
rows.imp_rat: Shear-sensitive component degradation from agitation reduces potency/quality; relevant with high severity for affected products.
rows.sat_rat: Actual degradation from agitation is hard to predict and only partially controlled by conservative specifications. Confirming low-shear performance requires added agitation trials. Interpreting degradation-versus-shear behavior needs scarce formulation knowledge. Degraded shear-sensitive material is costly product loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that evidence used for batch disposition is inaccurate or unverifiable
rows.plain: Make sure the information used to decide whether a batch can be released is correct and can be checked. Unreliable information could lead to releasing a batch that should not be used.
rows.imp_rat: Inaccurate or unverifiable disposition evidence can release a defective batch; low mixing relevance but severe compliance/safety consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Audit trails and review-by-second-person practices help, but data-integrity lapses remain a leading cause of regulatory findings, so evidence trustworthiness is only partially assured. Verifying evidence integrity adds review time but is not primarily a delay problem. Data-integrity review demands trained reviewers who can spot unverifiable or altered records. Audit-trail systems and review labor add cost but are standard GMP spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the compounding cycle exceeds the allowable sterile hold window
rows.plain: Make sure the mixing process finishes before the allowed time for safely holding the sterile material runs out. Going beyond that time could make the material unsuitable for use.
rows.imp_rat: Exceeding the allowable sterile hold window compromises sterility of the whole batch; moderate mixing relevance but severe consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Hold-window compliance is manageable when cycles are well characterized but risky when cycle times vary unexpectedly. Comparing cycle time against sterile hold window is a defined scheduling check. Estimating total cycle against hold limits needs process planning skill. Exceeding hold windows forces batch discard, a major cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of heat-sensitive component degradation caused by localized thermal exposure during compounding
rows.plain: Reduce damage to ingredients or parts that can be harmed by heat during mixing. This helps preserve their quality and effectiveness.
rows.imp_rat: Localized thermal exposure degrading heat-sensitive components harms quality; relevant with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Wall hot spots causing degradation are a known risk that scraped agitation and control partly but not fully mitigate. Assessing localized thermal exposure requires added characterization. Identifying hot-spot degradation risk needs specialized thermal-sensitivity knowledge. Heat-degraded material is wasted active.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required dissolution and blending conditions lacking evidence of reproducible results
rows.plain: Make sure there is evidence that the required mixing and dissolving conditions produce the same reliable results each time. This helps show that the process works consistently.
rows.imp_rat: Conditions lacking reproducibility evidence weaken validation confidence; relevant to reliable job delivery with high compliance severity.
rows.sat_rat: Established DQ/IQ/OQ/PQ frameworks and vendor test packages cover most reproducibility conditions, yet gaps in specific edge conditions routinely persist. Assembling reproducibility evidence across conditions takes weeks of protocol review, though templates from prior qualifications shorten this. Judging whether replicate runs constitute adequate evidence demands experienced validation engineers who are in short supply. Repeat qualification runs consume material and vendor time, but standard budgets absorb this expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between measured component concentrations and actual batch concentrations
rows.plain: Make sure the measured amounts of ingredients are as close as possible to the amounts actually present in the production mixture. This helps confirm that the mixture has the intended makeup.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between measured and actual concentrations undermines the reliability of disposition data; severe because it can mask an out-of-spec batch.
rows.sat_rat: Validated assays, calibrated instruments, and control charts make measured-versus-actual concentration agreement one of the best-controlled aspects of compounding today. Assay turnaround for concentration verification runs hours to days, but the governance concern is accuracy of the result rather than its speed. Validated analytical methods and trained QC analysts make concentration measurement a mature, repeatable competency for the overseer to rely on. Reference standards and instrument time cost money, but this is a routine budgeted expense rather than a pain point.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of formulation temperature excursion caused by the order or pace of exothermic additions
rows.plain: Keep the mixture from getting too hot or too cold because ingredients are added too quickly or in the wrong order. This helps protect the mixture and keep the process under control.
rows.imp_rat: Temperature excursion from exothermic addition order can degrade product or cause boil-off; relevant with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Controlled dosing rates and jacket cooling usually contain excursions, but fast or manual additions still occasionally spike temperature. Managing exothermic-addition pace to avoid excursions may require slowed, longer charging. Pacing exothermic additions safely depends on operator knowledge of the reaction. An uncontrolled exotherm can degrade material and waste a batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that translated mixing parameters exceed vessel operating limits at the target volume
rows.plain: Make sure the adjusted mixing settings stay within what the vessel can safely handle when it contains the intended amount. This helps prevent unsafe operation or poor mixing.
rows.imp_rat: Translated parameters exceeding vessel limits at target volume risks equipment/process failure; relevant with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Engineering review usually catches limit violations before execution, though scale-up translation errors do slip through. Catching parameter translations that exceed vessel limits at scale is a design-time check, not a runtime delay. Correctly translating parameters to the target volume demands scale-up engineering expertise. Parameters exceeding limits can damage equipment or scrap batches, an expensive failure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of batch temperature deviation from the validated mixing range
rows.plain: Keep the mixture close to the approved temperature range while it is being mixed. This helps the batch turn out as expected.
rows.imp_rat: Temperature deviation from validated range risks dissolution failure and degradation; relevant with high severity for product quality.
rows.sat_rat: Automated jacket temperature control keeps batches within validated range reliably in most facilities. Bringing and holding the batch in the validated range can add ramp and equilibration time. Tuning jacket control to stay in range needs modest operator skill supported by automation. Out-of-range temperature risks batch loss and energy overuse.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that product or cleaning residues remain on vessel product-contact surfaces after cleaning
rows.plain: Make sure as little product or cleaning material as possible is left on the surfaces that touch the product after cleaning. This helps prevent unwanted substances from getting into the product.
rows.imp_rat: Residues remaining after cleaning risk cross-contamination of the next batch; low direct mixing relevance but catastrophic contamination severity.
rows.sat_rat: Validated CIP with defined surface finishes reliably clears residues for most well-characterized products, yet tenacious actives and worst-case residues still produce occasional failures that keep this from being fully solved. Automated CIP cycles run in defined timeframes, but confirming true residue removal still requires swab and rinse sampling turnaround that adds hours to days. Selecting electropolished 316L surfaces and validated CIP recipes is well understood by qualified engineers, but interpreting residue-limit adequacy across new formulations still demands specialist cleaning-validation judgement. Meeting stringent residue limits drives investment in high-finish surfaces and analytical testing, but this is a normalized, budgeted cost in pharma capital projects.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a candidate vessel cannot meet required heating and cooling profiles under maximum thermal load
rows.plain: Make sure a proposed vessel can heat and cool the material as required even when it is carrying the greatest expected heat load. This helps confirm that the equipment can handle the process in demanding conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Meeting heating/cooling profiles under max thermal load supports dissolution but is a thermal-capability adjunct; severe for exothermic or heat-sensitive formulations.
rows.sat_rat: Established heat-transfer engineering usually predicts profiles well, though worst-case thermal load fouling and viscosity effects create residual misses. Heat-transfer calculations from jacket area and coolant data are quick once inputs are known. Thermal duty sizing is a well-codified engineering discipline requiring moderate but common expertise. Oversizing jackets or utilities to hedge uncertainty adds capital cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of batch-to-batch variation in reaching the specified dissolution and homogeneity endpoint
rows.plain: Make sure every batch reaches the required level of dissolving and even mixing in about the same way. This makes the process more predictable and the results more consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Batch-to-batch variation in reaching dissolution/homogeneity endpoint reflects process robustness; relevant with moderate-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Batch-to-batch endpoint variation persists without tight process analytical control. Confirming endpoint consistency across batches takes repeated cycles. Diagnosing endpoint variation causes needs experienced judgement. Endpoint variability drives inconsistent cycle times and losses.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical dissolution, blend-uniformity, and temperature-control outputs lacking reproducibility limits
rows.plain: Make sure important test results for mixing, dissolving, and temperature control can be repeated reliably and have clear limits for acceptable variation. This helps people know when the process is working properly.
rows.imp_rat: Missing reproducibility limits for critical outputs is a specification-completeness gap; moderately relevant with notable compliance severity.
rows.sat_rat: Critical outputs are usually specified but reproducibility limits are inconsistently applied, leaving recurring undefined bounds. Defining reproducibility limits for each critical output extends protocol development but follows known templates. Setting statistically defensible limits for dissolution, blend uniformity, and temperature control requires scarce validation and statistics expertise. Deriving limits mainly costs analyst hours rather than materials.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of batch-to-batch variation in undissolved component mass at the specified endpoint
rows.plain: Keep the amount of material that remains undissolved at the chosen stopping point nearly the same from one batch to the next. This makes the process and its results more dependable.
rows.imp_rat: Batch-to-batch variation in undissolved mass reflects dissolution inconsistency; relevant with moderate-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Batch-to-batch dissolution variation persists where endpoint control is not tightly instrumented. Tracking batch-to-batch undissolved mass requires ongoing sampling over cycles. Distinguishing variation causes needs experienced process interpretation. Variable undissolved mass causes inconsistent yield losses.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of formulation component degradation caused by charging before suitable vessel conditions are established
rows.plain: Ingredients should not be added until the container is in the right condition. This helps prevent the ingredients from being damaged or becoming less effective.
rows.imp_rat: Degradation from charging before suitable conditions harms product quality; relevant with high severity for sensitive components.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe entry conditions and interlocks usually prevent premature charging, with occasional lapses. Waiting for suitable vessel conditions before charging can delay the batch. Knowing when conditions are suitable relies on operator judgement and interlocks. Premature charging degrades material, causing loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variation in dissolution endpoint achievement across repeat batches
rows.plain: Repeated batches should reach the point where ingredients have fully dissolved to the same extent each time. This helps make the product consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Variation in dissolution endpoint across repeat batches undermines process consistency; relevant with moderate-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Repeat-batch dissolution data is routinely collected in PQ, giving moderate confidence though variation still appears. Running repeat batches to characterize endpoint variation adds substantial campaign duration. Interpreting endpoint variation across runs requires analytical and process judgement. Replicate batches consume active material, a real recurring expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of heat from dissolution or agitation exceeding available jacket heat removal
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that mixing or dissolving the material creates more heat than the equipment can remove. Keeping the temperature under control helps protect the process and its product.
rows.imp_rat: Dissolution/agitation heat exceeding jacket removal causes runaway temperature and degradation; relevant to thermal control with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Jacket capacity is typically sized with margin, so exceeding removal capability is uncommon but possible with high loads. If dissolution heat outpaces jacket removal, cooling holds extend the batch. Anticipating heat-load-versus-capacity mismatch requires engineering knowledge of the system. Insufficient heat removal can degrade material and force scrap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of consequential vessel geometry and working-volume effects omitted from mixing capability requirements
rows.plain: Make sure mixing requirements account for how the container’s shape and fill level affect the result. This helps ensure the contents are mixed properly.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted geometry/working-volume effects in requirements risk dead zones and poor mixing; relevant upstream with moderate-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Consequential baffle, aspect-ratio and low-fill effects are commonly omitted, producing unreliable capability requirements. Accounting for geometry and working-volume effects adds analysis steps to the spec process. Recognizing which geometry and impeller-to-volume effects matter demands deep mixing expertise that most influencers lack. Overlooked geometry effects cause redesign or dead-zone remediation costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of utility interruptions that stop vessel mixing or thermal control
rows.plain: Reduce interruptions to the services that keep the vessel mixing and at the right temperature. This helps prevent delays and keeps the production schedule on track.
rows.imp_rat: Utility interruptions stopping mixing/thermal control can abort or ruin a batch; relevant with moderate-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Redundant utilities and preventive maintenance reduce frequency, but interruptions still occur and are largely outside overseer control. Utility interruptions halt mixing and thermal control, causing unplanned stoppages that extend the batch. Restoring utilities is a facilities task not dependent on compounding operator skill. Interruptions risk batch loss and idle capacity, a meaningful cost exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical formulation and process inputs missing from the mixing requirements basis
rows.plain: Make sure the mixing plan includes all important product and process information. This helps ensure the equipment and mixing method are suitable for making the product correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Missing critical formulation/process inputs in the requirements basis is an upstream ambiguity that can misdirect the whole design; moderately relevant with meaningful severity.
rows.sat_rat: Requirements baskets are chronically incomplete because early-stage formulation data is immature and no standard checklist reliably captures every critical input. Chasing missing formulation inputs from R&D lengthens the requirements-gathering phase considerably. Knowing which inputs are critical demands cross-functional formulation and process knowledge that is unevenly distributed. Missing inputs surface late and drive costly rework or requoting.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that established mixing parameters exceed compounding vessel operating limits
rows.plain: Choose mixing settings that stay within what the mixing tank can safely handle. This helps prevent equipment damage or an unsafe process.
rows.imp_rat: Established parameters exceeding vessel operating limits risk mechanical failure or off-spec mixing; relevant with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Documented operating limits and reviews reliably prevent most limit exceedances. Verifying parameters stay within vessel limits is an up-front check with little runtime impact. Comparing parameters to vessel limits requires equipment knowledge but is well-supported by documentation. Exceeding limits risks equipment damage and lost batches.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of temperature variation across the batch during heating and cooling
rows.plain: Keep the temperature as even as possible throughout the batch while it is heated or cooled. This helps the whole batch have the same quality.
rows.imp_rat: Temperature variation during heating/cooling affects uniform dissolution and local degradation; moderately central with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Jacketed vessels with agitation give fairly uniform temperatures, though localized gradients remain in viscous or large batches. Thermal uniformity mapping adds bounded measurement time. Interpreting temperature gradients uses standard thermal engineering. Thermal nonuniformity can degrade material and waste energy.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the compounding vessel cannot hold a parameter within its proposed range under normal load
rows.plain: Make sure the mixing container can keep an important setting within its intended range during normal operation. This helps the process stay under control.
rows.imp_rat: A vessel that cannot hold a parameter in range under normal load is fundamentally unfit; relevant with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Vessels are usually qualified against their ranges, so inability to hold a parameter under normal load is uncommon. If the vessel cannot hold a parameter, batches stall or fail, adding recovery time. Anticipating vessel capability shortfalls needs engineering assessment. Capability shortfalls cause failed batches and material waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of the same vessel disruption recurring before batch completion
rows.plain: Prevent the same equipment or process problem from happening again before the batch is finished. This helps avoid repeated interruptions and delays.
rows.imp_rat: Recurring vessel disruptions before batch completion threaten completion and consistency; relevant with moderate-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Without strong in-batch corrective closure, the same disruption recurring before completion remains a real, poorly-controlled risk. Recurring disruptions repeatedly interrupt the batch, cumulatively costing time. Preventing recurrence depends on effective root-cause skill that is not always applied mid-batch. Repeat disruptions waste resources and can jeopardize the batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of disagreement among homogeneity results for the same batch
rows.plain: Tests of the same batch should give results that are more alike. This helps show whether the batch is mixed evenly throughout.
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement among homogeneity results creates uncertainty in the release decision; relevant with high severity if a bad result is discounted.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized sampling and assay methods keep result agreement usually acceptable, but method and sampling variability still produce occasional discordance. Resolving disagreements among results requires resampling or retesting, adding delay. Interpreting discordant homogeneity results demands analytical judgement to distinguish real variation from assay error. Repeat testing to reconcile disagreement consumes assay and labor resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of bulk-solution degradation from blending beyond the approved endpoint
rows.plain: Avoid mixing the bulk product longer than approved, since this could cause it to break down or lose quality. This protects the product from damage during preparation.
rows.imp_rat: Degradation from over-blending past endpoint reduces quality; relevant with high severity for potency/impurity.
rows.sat_rat: Validated blend endpoints make degradation from overblending rare for characterized products. Defined mix endpoints prevent excessive blending that would otherwise waste time. Recognizing overblending risk relies on validated endpoints rather than operator vigilance, easing skill demand. Overblending degradation would waste an entire batch, but endpoints largely prevent it.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of variation in component dissolution behavior between material lots
rows.plain: Make the way the components dissolve more consistent from one batch of material to another. This helps the product behave predictably each time it is made.
rows.imp_rat: Variation in dissolution behavior between lots challenges endpoint consistency; relevant with moderate-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Incoming material specs limit but do not eliminate lot-driven dissolution differences, a recurring source of variation. Lot-to-lot dissolution variation forces extended or repeated mixing, adding time. Compensating for material-lot variability relies on experienced adjustment. Lot variability causing failures wastes material and forces rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of temperature nonuniformity among regions of the batch during mixing
rows.plain: Keep the temperature as even as possible throughout the mixture while it is being mixed. This helps ensure that all parts of the product are treated the same way.
rows.imp_rat: Temperature nonuniformity during mixing affects dissolution rate and local degradation; moderately central with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Jacketed heating with agitation gives fairly uniform temperature, but gradients near the wall and center still occur. Verifying thermal uniformity across regions takes multi-probe monitoring and settling time. Diagnosing regional temperature gradients requires thermal and mixing expertise. Hot or cold spots can degrade material or impair dissolution, wasting product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between mixing and thermal requirements across compounding stages
rows.plain: Make the mixing and heating or cooling needs fit together across all preparation steps. This helps the product be made consistently without one step interfering with another.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between mixing and thermal requirements across stages can leave a stage under-served; relevant with moderate-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Stage-to-stage mismatches are common where multi-step processes are specified with single-condition assumptions. Reconciling mixing and thermal needs across stages adds coordination time. Balancing competing stage-by-stage requirements demands integrated process expertise. Mismatches force compromise equipment or added utilities cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of temperature variation across the target-volume batch during mixing
rows.plain: Keep the temperature as even as possible throughout the full amount being mixed. This helps the mixture turn out consistently.
rows.imp_rat: Temperature variation across the batch affects uniform dissolution and degradation; moderately central with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Circulating agitation with jacket control usually holds uniform temperature, though large volumes retain minor gradients. Confirming batch-wide temperature uniformity adds monitoring time. Judging acceptable uniformity across the batch takes process experience. Temperature spread risks off-spec dissolution and material loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that mechanical or utility failure makes the vessel unavailable during a scheduled batch
rows.plain: Prevent equipment or service failures from making the vessel unavailable during a planned batch. This helps avoid delays and keeps production capacity available.
rows.imp_rat: Mechanical/utility failure making the vessel unavailable disrupts batch execution and hold windows; relevant with moderate-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Preventive maintenance programs make scheduled-batch vessel availability generally dependable, with occasional unexpected failures. An unavailable vessel forces rescheduling that consumes shift time. Preventing failure depends on maintenance expertise rather than overseer action. Vessel unavailability idles capacity and disrupts throughput, a significant cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an unplanned compounding-vessel failure stopping batch execution
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that the mixing vessel fails unexpectedly and stops the batch. This helps the batch finish on schedule.
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned vessel failure stopping execution can abort or spoil the batch; relevant with moderate-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Maintained jacketed vessels are generally robust, so mid-batch failures are uncommon but not eliminated. An unplanned failure stops execution and forces time-consuming recovery. Diagnosis and repair fall to maintenance specialists, not the overseer. Batch stoppage from equipment failure risks material loss and lost capacity.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of air entrained in the bulk solution during blending
rows.plain: Keep as much air as possible out of the liquid while it is being mixed. Too much trapped air can affect the quality of the mixture.
rows.imp_rat: Air entrainment affects blend quality and downstream fill accuracy; moderately relevant, with moderate severity as it can be corrected by hold/deaeration.
rows.sat_rat: Air entrainment is a chronic, hard-to-see problem with only partial mitigation via baffles and speed limits, so outcomes vary. Slow addition and speed control add time but the operator can pace charging to limit air pull-in. Avoiding vortex entrainment relies heavily on operator experience in judging speed and blade submergence. Entrained air rarely destroys batch material but can force re-blend or extended holds that waste time and energy.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of batch-to-batch variation in the product temperature trajectory under equivalent conditions
rows.plain: Keep the product's temperature changing in the same way from one batch to the next when conditions are the same. This makes the process more predictable and consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Batch-to-batch variation in temperature trajectory affects dissolution/degradation consistency; moderately relevant with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Modern jacket control gives repeatable trajectories, though fouling and load changes cause some drift. Verifying temperature-trajectory repeatability adds monitoring time. Interpreting trajectory variation uses standard control-engineering skill. Trajectory variation risks inconsistent quality and energy waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of deviation in blend temperature from the specified profile
rows.plain: Keep the mixture's temperature close to the required temperature pattern over time. This helps keep the product consistent from one production group to the next.
rows.imp_rat: Blend temperature deviation from profile affects dissolution and degradation; moderately relevant with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Programmed jacket control follows the profile reliably in most modern systems. Correcting deviations from the temperature profile adds control settling time. Following a specified thermal profile requires modest skill with automated support. Profile deviations can impair dissolution and waste material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical process and qualification requirements omitted from vessel-option evaluation
rows.plain: Leave out as few important process needs and required suitability checks as possible when comparing container options. This helps ensure the chosen container can support the work and meet all required checks.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted requirements from vessel-option evaluation is an upstream completeness gap; moderate relevance with meaningful severity.
rows.sat_rat: URS and risk-assessment practices capture most requirements, yet critical ones still slip through in complex vessel evaluations. Requirement capture upfront lengthens the evaluation start but prevents later rework. Identifying all critical process and qualification requirements needs cross-functional SME input that is hard to fully assemble. Requirement omission is cheap to prevent with review time versus costly to remediate later.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of contamination introduced by utilities contacting the vessel or batch
rows.plain: Keep contamination from entering through support services that touch the container or its contents, such as air or other connected supplies. This protects the batch from being spoiled by those connections.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination from utilities contacting the batch is a low-mixing-relevance but high-severity sterility risk.
rows.sat_rat: Qualified clean utilities, WFI loops and filtered gases make utility-borne contamination uncommon under GMP. Utility qualification and checks are done upfront and rarely delay compounding. Ensuring clean utilities depends on engineering controls rather than operator skill. Contamination from utilities is rare but a single event can scrap an entire batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a batch is transferred to filling before final disposition authorization is communicated
rows.plain: Do not send a batch to the filling step until the final approval to release it has been communicated. This prevents work from starting on a batch that has not yet been approved.
rows.imp_rat: Transferring to filling before disposition authorization is a procedural/quality-gate breach rather than a mixing-mechanics failure; severe compliance consequence if defective batch escapes.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic batch record status locks and quarantine holds in modern MES make premature transfer rare, though manual paper systems leave residual gaps. Disposition-to-transfer sequencing is gated by hold procedures, so the timing control is generally enforced rather than a delay issue. Following the release-before-transfer rule requires procedural discipline more than specialized skill. Physical and electronic quarantine controls carry modest cost that is well absorbed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required qualification evidence missing from each vessel option's evaluation package
rows.plain: Make sure the evaluation file for each possible container includes all required supporting evidence. This helps people compare the options and choose one with enough proof behind it.
rows.imp_rat: Missing qualification evidence weakens the vessel selection package; moderately relevant with notable compliance severity.
rows.sat_rat: Structured qualification checklists catch most required evidence, though missing items still surface late in review. Compiling the full qualification package is slow when vendor documents arrive piecemeal. Knowing which evidence is required draws on GMP-experienced reviewers using checklists that partly offset expert scarcity. Chasing missing documents adds coordination cost but no major material outlay.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sequence steps lacking entry conditions for temperature, pH, agitation, or prior dissolution
rows.plain: Make sure each step says what temperature, acidity, mixing, and earlier dissolving must be in place before it starts. This matters because missing starting conditions can make the process inconsistent or unsafe.
rows.imp_rat: Sequence steps lacking entry conditions creates process ambiguity that can cause precipitation or incomplete dissolution; moderately relevant with moderate-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Modern electronic recipes embed entry conditions, but legacy or manual procedures still have gaps. Missing entry conditions cause mid-sequence stops while conditions are checked, adding time. Recognizing missing entry conditions requires process understanding to author complete recipes. Steps run without proper entry conditions can spoil batches and waste material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that batch disposition applies obsolete homogeneity or GMP acceptance criteria
rows.plain: Avoid deciding whether a batch is acceptable using old or conflicting rules about how evenly mixed it must be and what quality standards it must meet. This matters because the decision may be wrong or out of date.
rows.imp_rat: Applying obsolete acceptance criteria at disposition is a compliance/governance error; low mixing relevance but severe if non-conforming batch released.
rows.sat_rat: Controlled document systems reduce use of superseded criteria, but version-control lapses and manual reference to outdated specs remain a recurring reliability gap. Criteria updates propagate slowly through document control, creating windows where obsolete specs could be applied. Judging which acceptance criteria version applies requires trained quality staff familiar with change control. Document-management systems add overhead but are standard infrastructure.
