rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of gas entrainment or foaming caused by candidate agitation under specified operating conditions
rows.plain: Choose mixing equipment that does not pull too much air into the liquid or create too much foam under the expected working conditions. This helps keep the mixture consistent and usable.
rows.imp_rat: Gas entrainment/foaming from candidate agitation affects blend and fill accuracy; moderately relevant with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Foaming under agitation is hard to predict at spec time and often surfaces only in production. Testing for foaming under operating conditions adds trial time. Predicting foaming behavior needs surfactant and interfacial expertise. Foaming causes fill errors and product loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of batches with gas entrainment or foaming above the specified limit during agitation
rows.plain: Reduce how often batches contain more trapped gas or foam than the allowed limit while being stirred. This matters because too much gas or foam can make the process unreliable.
rows.imp_rat: Batches exceeding gas-entrainment/foaming limits affect blend and fill; moderately relevant with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Foaming excursion frequency remains a recurring, imperfectly controlled defect. Tracking foaming frequency across batches requires ongoing monitoring. Diagnosing foaming excursions needs specialized interfacial knowledge. Foaming batches above limit cause repeated losses.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of gas entrained in the batch during agitation
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of air or other gas mixed into the batch while it is being stirred. This matters because trapped gas can affect the batch and how well the process works.
rows.imp_rat: Gas entrained during agitation affects blend quality and fill accuracy; moderately relevant with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Gas entrainment is inconsistently controlled and often only discovered during production runs. Measuring entrained gas adds specific test time. Controlling entrainment through impeller/level design needs mixing expertise. Entrained gas causes fill and assay errors, wasting product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of agitator-speed drift outside the approved range during steady mixing
rows.plain: Keep the mixing speed within the approved range while the mixture is being stirred steadily. Stable speed helps keep the mixture consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Agitator-speed drift outside range during steady mixing affects blend consistency; moderately relevant with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Closed-loop VFD control holds agitator speed steadily within range in most installations. Speed monitoring is continuous and adds no time. Maintaining steady speed is automated on VFD systems, needing little operator skill. Minor speed drift seldom causes material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of mixing conditions outside defined ranges during compounding
rows.plain: Keep the mixing process within its required limits while ingredients are being combined. This helps ensure the finished mixture is evenly blended.
rows.imp_rat: Duration of mixing conditions outside defined ranges risks incomplete dissolution/degradation; moderately relevant with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Automated setpoint control keeps mixing conditions in range most of the time, with brief transients. Duration spent with mixing conditions out of range prolongs the effective process time. Keeping conditions in range depends on control skill plus alarm response. Out-of-range mixing time wastes energy and may spoil product.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of vessel contents remaining outside the prescribed dissolution temperature range
rows.plain: Keep the contents of the vessel within the required temperature range for dissolving them. This helps avoid slowing down the process or affecting the result.
rows.imp_rat: Duration outside dissolution temperature range slows the job and risks incomplete dissolution; moderately relevant, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Automated control returns temperature to range fairly consistently, keeping out-of-range periods short in most batches. Time spent outside the dissolution temperature range directly lengthens the productive dissolution window and total batch time. Minimizing out-of-range duration relies on operator responsiveness and thermal control skill. Extended out-of-range time wastes energy and can spoil material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of unstable batch temperature oscillations during mixing
rows.plain: Keep the batch temperature from repeatedly rising and falling outside a steady pattern during mixing. This helps the batch remain consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Unstable temperature oscillations during mixing affect dissolution consistency and degradation; moderately relevant with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Properly tuned control loops rarely oscillate, but poorly tuned or aggressive setpoints still produce hunting. Oscillations force operators to wait for stabilization, adding time. Diagnosing and damping oscillations requires control-loop expertise. Oscillation-driven excursions can waste energy and risk material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of inaccessible or poorly drained regions in the vessel geometry
rows.plain: Make the vessel's shape easier to clean and drain completely, with fewer hard-to-reach or water-trapping areas. This helps prevent residue from being left behind.
rows.imp_rat: Inaccessible/poorly drained regions cause dead zones and cleaning residues; moderately relevant with moderate-high severity for uniformity and contamination.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized hygienic-design rules, sanitary fittings, and spray-ball coverage testing reliably minimize inaccessible regions, making this one of the better-served needs though sensor ports and complex internals still create residual risk. Riboflavin coverage tests and drainage checks are routine but add commissioning time when dead legs are discovered late. Recognizing and eliminating dead legs, crevices, and poor slopes is a mature hygienic-design competency embedded in ASME BPE practice and vendor design reviews. Hygienic-design geometry costs more in fabrication but avoids far larger downstream cleaning-failure costs, so it is treated as standard spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time required for the target-volume batch to meet dissolution and homogeneity criteria
rows.plain: Reduce the time needed for a batch of the intended size to dissolve fully and become evenly mixed. This helps the product reach the required quality sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Time to meet dissolution/homogeneity is a throughput concern, moderately relevant to the job's completion; delay consequences are cost/schedule rather than safety.
rows.sat_rat: Fixed validated times give consistent outcomes but are deliberately padded, so true minimum time is seldom achieved. Meeting dissolution and homogeneity in minimum time is the core throughput lever, yet mix times are usually set conservatively long to be safe. Trimming mix time without risking failure demands expert process understanding rarely fully available. Excess mixing time consumes energy and ties up the vessel, adding operating cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of mixing beyond the planned compounding window before dissolution criteria are met
rows.plain: Prevent mixing from taking longer than the planned preparation period before the mixture dissolves enough. This helps the batch stay on schedule.
rows.imp_rat: Mixing beyond the planned window before dissolution is met is a delay/hold-time concern; moderate relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Conservative fixed times usually reach endpoint but often overshoot, so overrun is common rather than rare. Mixing beyond the planned window before endpoint is met is pure added batch time. Judging when to stop safely depends on operator experience with endpoint signs. Overmixing wastes energy and vessel time and can shear-damage material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of dissolution beyond the planned compounding interval because of ingredient characteristics
rows.plain: Prevent the mixture from taking longer than planned to dissolve because of the ingredients being used. This helps keep preparation on schedule.
rows.imp_rat: Extended dissolution from ingredient characteristics is a delay concern for throughput and hold time; moderate relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Validated processes make eventual dissolution reliable, but the time overrun itself is the recurring problem. Difficult-to-dissolve ingredients routinely extend dissolution beyond the planned interval, a familiar schedule pressure. Managing slow dissolution relies on operator adjustments (temperature, agitation) grounded in experience. Extended dissolution consumes extra vessel time and energy, tying up capacity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required for the batch to reach composition acceptance limits under worst-case intended conditions
rows.plain: Get the batch evenly mixed and within the allowed ingredient ranges as quickly as possible, even under the most difficult expected conditions. This helps ensure every part of the batch has the intended makeup.
rows.imp_rat: Time to reach composition limits under worst case is a delay/throughput concern; moderate relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Blend-time estimation is fairly established, though worst-case conditions introduce prediction uncertainty. Batch cycle time to reach acceptance under worst-case is a well-tracked and optimizable parameter. Estimating worst-case blend time requires moderate mixing expertise. Longer cycles consume utilities and reduce throughput.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of powder escaping into the surrounding environment during vessel charging
rows.plain: Keep as much powder as possible from escaping into the surrounding area when it is added to a vessel. This reduces wasted material and helps keep the work area cleaner and safer.
rows.imp_rat: Powder escaping during charging is a yield/loss and operator-exposure concern; moderate relevance and severity, higher for potent compounds.
rows.sat_rat: Open manual charging routinely generates fugitive dust with only partial control by hoods or booths. Careful manual charging to limit dust adds time to the charging step. Dust-free powder addition demands considerable operator technique when open charging. Escaped powder is lost active/excipient plus a cleaning and containment cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required gas-entrainment and foaming limits omitted from mixing requirements
rows.plain: Include all necessary limits for how much gas can be drawn into the mixture and how much foam can form. This helps ensure the mixing instructions produce a usable product.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted gas-entrainment/foaming limits in requirements is a specification gap; moderately relevant with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Gas-entrainment and foaming constraints are frequently left out of mixing requirements, a common oversight. Adding foaming and gas-entrainment limits to requirements takes extra spec effort. Knowing to specify entrainment limits requires niche awareness often absent. Omitted limits lead to costly foaming rework post-installation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of air entrained in the preparation under the established mixing conditions
rows.plain: Keep the amount of air mixed into the preparation as low as possible while using the agreed mixing settings. This helps protect the preparation’s intended quality.
rows.imp_rat: Air entrained under established mixing conditions affects blend and fill accuracy; moderately relevant with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Sub-surface addition and controlled agitation limit entrainment, but vortexing still introduces air in some setups. Waiting for entrained air to disengage before endpoint extends the batch. Avoiding air entrainment depends on operator control of agitation and level. Entrained air can spoil fill accuracy and cause rework cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of agitation-speed overshoot above the approved upper limit during startup or adjustment
rows.plain: Keep the mixing speed from rising above its approved upper limit when starting or adjusting the equipment. This helps keep the product under controlled conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Agitation-speed overshoot at startup can shear-damage or foam; moderately relevant with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: VFDs with soft-start and speed limits make overshoot uncommon on modern drives but manual units still drift. Controlled ramping adds negligible time. Preventing overshoot leans on operator attention with manual controls or on drive tuning. Overshoot rarely wastes material but can stress equipment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time for batch temperature to return to the validated range after additions or agitation changes
rows.plain: Help the product temperature return to its approved range quickly after ingredients are added or the mixture is stirred differently. This keeps the process stable and consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Recovery time to validated temperature range is a throughput/stability delay; moderate relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Feedback jacket control brings temperature back consistently, though lag in large volumes lengthens recovery. Recovery time back to range after additions or agitation changes is a direct delay in the batch. Speeding recovery depends on control tuning skill more than daily operator finesse. Longer recovery consumes energy and vessel occupancy cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of deviation in mixing intensity from the batch target
rows.plain: Keep the strength of mixing close to the planned level for the batch. This helps make sure the ingredients are mixed as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Mixing-intensity deviation from target affects blend consistency; moderately relevant with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Variable-speed drives hold set agitation consistently, so intensity deviation is uncommon. Correcting mixing-intensity deviation is quick but confirming it can add checks. Setting and holding target agitation is straightforward with calibrated drives. Wrong intensity risks poor blend or shear damage, wasting material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of deviation in total mixing exposure from the batch target
rows.plain: Keep the total time and strength of mixing close to the planned amount for the batch. This helps produce a consistent result from one batch to the next.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation in total mixing exposure from target affects degradation and dissolution completeness; moderately relevant with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-driven timers control total exposure reliably in most systems. Total mixing exposure is timer-controlled, so deviations are caught but may extend batches. Tracking cumulative exposure needs little skill with automated timers. Excess exposure wastes energy; too little risks rework cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of batch material ejected by foaming or splashing during agitation
rows.plain: Limit how much batch material is thrown out of the equipment by bubbles or splashing during mixing.
rows.imp_rat: Material ejected by foaming/splashing is a yield/waste loss with potency implications; moderate relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Foaming behavior varies by formulation and is only partly controlled by baffles and speed limits. Ramping agitation gently to avoid splash costs some setup time but is manageable. Preventing foam and splash depends on operator finesse with speed and antifoam addition. Ejected material is a direct product loss and foaming can waste significant batch value.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of component material retained on containers and charging implements after charging
rows.plain: Leave as little of the material behind as possible on containers and tools after they are filled. This helps prevent waste and ensures the intended amount reaches the process.
rows.imp_rat: Component retained on containers/implements is a charging-accuracy and yield concern that can shift potency; moderate relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Retention amounts vary with material and technique, causing minor yield inconsistency. Rinsing containers and implements adds handling time but is standard practice. Quantitative transfer and rinsing require care but are routine operator tasks. Material clinging to containers and scoops is a recurring direct loss only partly recovered by rinsing.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of batches requiring parameter rework because component properties fall outside establishment assumptions
rows.plain: Reduce how often a batch needs its settings changed because the materials do not behave as expected. This saves time and makes the mixing process more predictable.
rows.imp_rat: Parameter rework from properties outside assumptions is a rework/throughput burden; moderate relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Establishment assumptions hold for most lots, but property drift occasionally triggers rework. Rework from out-of-assumption component properties adds substantial batch delay. Diagnosing and adjusting for off-assumption properties requires strong process expertise. Parameter rework wastes materials and labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of cycle-completion variation across expected material properties and batch loads
rows.plain: Make the results at the end of each run as consistent as possible, even when the materials or batch sizes vary. This helps each batch finish properly.
rows.imp_rat: Cycle-completion variation across material properties/loads affects throughput predictability; moderate relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Cycle-time variation across loads and properties is only loosely characterized at spec time. Cycle-completion time variation across materials and loads is measurable but hard to bound. Predicting cycle variation across the material range needs process expertise. Variable cycles disrupt scheduling and utilities budgeting.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of liquid batch material spilled or leaked during charging, compounding, or transfer
rows.plain: Prevent as much liquid material as possible from being spilled or leaked while it is added, mixed, or moved. This reduces waste and helps keep people and the work area safe.
rows.imp_rat: Spilled or leaked batch material is a yield/safety/waste concern; moderate relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Closed connections and drip trays reduce but do not eliminate spillage variability. Cleaning up spills interrupts flow but is infrequent. Careful charging and transfer connections require attentive handling that most operators manage. Spills and leaks are direct material and cleanup losses that occur intermittently.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of active or excipient material adhering to vessel surfaces during blending and hold
rows.plain: Keep as little drug ingredient or added ingredient as possible from sticking to the inside of the container during mixing and holding. This helps keep the mixture evenly blended and preserves the intended amount of each ingredient.
rows.imp_rat: Material adhering to vessel surfaces is a yield/waste concern that can also shift potency; moderate relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Good vessel design and agitation limit adhesion, but sticky formulations still leave residue on surfaces. Material adhering to surfaces prolongs cleaning and can extend mixing to recover it. Reducing wall adhesion depends on knowledge of wetting and surface effects. Adhered material is lost yield and increases cleaning cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of endpoint delay caused by foam or entrained air in the blend
rows.plain: Shorten delays at the planned finish point caused by foam or trapped air in the mixture. This helps the process finish on time.
rows.imp_rat: Endpoint delay from foam/entrained air is a throughput concern; moderate relevance and low-moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: De-aeration practices work but foam-prone formulations still cause unpredictable delays. Foam or entrained air delays reaching a confirmable endpoint, directly extending time. Managing and de-aerating foam takes operator technique and antifoam knowledge. De-aeration holds waste vessel time and energy.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of batches requiring repeat mixing after failed homogeneity checks
rows.plain: Reduce how often a batch must be mixed again because its contents were not evenly distributed. This helps batches finish on schedule.
rows.imp_rat: Repeat mixing after failed homogeneity checks is rework — a cost/throughput and hold-time burden, not a direct safety escape; moderate relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Failed checks still occur often enough that remix cycles are a familiar recurring event rather than a rarity. Repeat mixing after a failed check directly consumes extra shift time and delays handoff. Deciding remix parameters relies on operator experience rather than automated guidance. Repeat mixing wastes energy and vessel occupancy, and is a recurring visible cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of batches requiring repeat mixing after failed homogeneity criteria
rows.plain: Reduce how often a batch must be mixed again because its contents were not evenly distributed. This frees equipment and time for other work.
rows.imp_rat: Repeat mixing after failed homogeneity criteria is rework driving cost and hold-time risk; moderate relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Validated parameters reduce failures but marginal batches still routinely require a second mixing pass. Remixing to meet failed criteria eats into the scheduled batch window. Corrective remix relies on established recipes but still needs judgement on additional mix duration. Each remix consumes vessel time and utilities, a tangible recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of thermal transitions between required batch temperatures
rows.plain: Shorten the time needed to heat or cool the batch between its required temperatures. This helps the production cycle run more efficiently.
rows.imp_rat: Duration of thermal transitions between temperatures is a delay/throughput concern; moderate relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Transition times are predictable with standard heat-transfer sizing, giving fairly dependable results. Thermal transition duration is directly measurable and a common optimization target. Sizing for transition speed uses established heat-transfer engineering. Slow transitions cost throughput and utility energy.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of batch material remaining as an unrecoverable vessel heel after transfer
rows.plain: Leave as little material as possible stuck in the container after the batch is moved out, so less product is wasted.
rows.imp_rat: Unrecoverable vessel heel is a yield-loss/waste concern; low-moderate relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Heel volume is fairly predictable per vessel, so it is consistent even if wasteful. Draining and heel recovery add transfer time that is largely accepted as routine. Minimizing heel depends on vessel design and operator drainage technique more than special skill. The unrecoverable heel is a persistent direct material loss on every batch with limited remedy.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of component stratification in the bulk solution before transfer
rows.imp_rat: Stratification before transfer directly opposes homogeneity (relevance), yet consequences are correctable; held at cap.
rows.sat_rat: Ongoing agitation reliably keeps most solutions blended right up to transfer. Continued agitation until transfer prevents stratification and costs modest time. Maintaining suspension is largely automated once parameters are set. Stratification risks non-uniform transfer and possible rejection.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the blend-endpoint sample misrepresents the vessel contents
rows.imp_rat: Blend-endpoint sample misrepresenting contents can pass a bad batch (relevance/severity); held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Endpoint sampling can misrepresent homogeneity if locations are poorly chosen. Multiple-location sampling to represent the blend adds time. Correct blend sampling technique requires training. A misrepresentative blend sample can pass a non-uniform batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of charging a component with an incorrect identity into the batch
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect-identity component charged ruins the batch (severity) and defeats correct blend (relevance); bounded by cap.
rows.sat_rat: Dispensing checks and label reconciliation make incorrect-identity charging uncommon. Identity check at charging adds brief time. Verifying component identity is procedural label checking. Wrong-identity charge can scrap the batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which the charged quantity deviates from the batch requirement
rows.imp_rat: Charged-quantity deviation directly changes concentration (relevance) and can ruin potency (severity); bounded by cap.
rows.sat_rat: Calibrated scales with double-checks keep charged quantities close to requirement reliably. Weighing to target adds routine time at charging. Accurate weighing needs care but is standard operator practice. Wrong charged quantity can scrap or dilute a batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of microbial or particulate contamination entering the bulk solution during charging
rows.imp_rat: Contamination entering bulk during charging is a genuine safety risk (severity); relevance moderate; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Open charging remains a real contamination exposure only partly mitigated by hoods and gowning. Careful, protected charging adds handling time. Preventing contamination during open charging demands trained technique. Contamination during charging can scrap a batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of charging a component into the wrong vessel or batch
rows.imp_rat: Charging into the wrong vessel/batch corrupts product identity (severity); relevance moderate; held at cap.
rows.sat_rat: Vessel labeling and status boards make wrong-vessel charging uncommon but human error persists. Vessel/batch verification is a quick pre-charge check. Confirming correct vessel requires attention, especially in multi-vessel suites. Charging the wrong vessel can scrap two batches.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of component agglomeration during introduction into the vessel
rows.imp_rat: Agglomeration at introduction impedes dissolution centrally, but it is typically recoverable through continued mixing; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Lump formation is a common, formulation-dependent problem only partly mitigated by sprinkling and high shear. Slow, dispersed addition to avoid lumps lengthens charging. Preventing agglomeration during addition is a technique-heavy manual task dependent on operator finesse. Agglomerates can trap undissolved active, risking yield and rework cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the endpoint is confirmed while vessel contents remain outside acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Confirming endpoint while contents are out of spec releases a non-conforming batch (severity), directly defeating the CFJ (relevance); held at cap.
rows.sat_rat: Defined criteria help but subjective confirmation can still pass out-of-spec contents. Confirming acceptance criteria before endpoint adds review time. Interpreting acceptance criteria correctly needs trained judgement. A wrongly confirmed endpoint can release a bad batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that endpoint evidence misrepresents the full vessel contents
rows.imp_rat: Endpoint evidence misrepresenting full contents can wrongly pass a non-homogeneous batch (relevance/severity); held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Single-point grab samples can misrepresent large vessels despite defined locations. Representative sampling to confirm endpoint adds sampling time. Ensuring the sample reflects full contents needs skilled technique. A misrepresentative endpoint can pass a bad batch, a big loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of change in vessel contents between endpoint sampling and bulk transfer
rows.imp_rat: Change between endpoint sampling and transfer can undermine the homogeneity job, but it is a narrow late-stage window; capped per ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Validated hold times and continued gentle agitation keep post-sample change small and predictable. Prompt transfer after sampling limits the change window and is usually scheduled tightly. Little special skill is needed beyond following hold-time limits. Excess change can force resampling or rejection with material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing microbial or particulate contamination during endpoint sampling
rows.imp_rat: Contamination introduced during endpoint sampling can spoil a finished batch (severity); relevance moderate; held at cap.
rows.sat_rat: Sampling ports and aseptic technique reduce but do not eliminate sampling-borne contamination. Aseptic sampling adds careful, slow technique time. Contamination-free endpoint sampling demands strong aseptic skill. A contaminated sample event can scrap the batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that changing bulk-solution viscosity pushes agitator load outside approved limits
rows.imp_rat: Rising viscosity pushing agitator load out of limits can stall mixing or damage equipment (relevance/severity); held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Load monitoring and validated speeds usually keep agitator load in range for known formulations. Monitoring load during viscosity change adds no time. Anticipating load rise needs some process knowledge. Overload can trip or damage the agitator.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of agitator seal leakage contaminating the bulk solution during mixing
rows.imp_rat: Seal leakage contaminating bulk during mixing is a real product-integrity hazard (severity); relevance moderate; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Mechanical seals and sanitary designs make seal-borne contamination during mixing uncommon. Seal integrity is maintained by design, not runtime effort. Detecting seal leakage needs some operator vigilance. Seal leakage contamination can scrap a batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of adding a component intended for a different sequence step
rows.imp_rat: Adding a component out of sequence can impair dissolution/homogeneity (relevance) with moderate consequence; at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Sequenced records reduce but manual execution still allows step-mixups. Checking the step before adding costs little time. Tracking sequence position requires operator attention and memory. Adding a wrong-step component can scrap the batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of starting the next addition before preceding-step dissolution or blending criteria are met
rows.imp_rat: Starting the next addition before prior dissolution is met directly harms homogeneity (relevance/severity); held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Visual endpoint judgement is subjective, so premature next additions still occur. Waiting to confirm dissolution before next addition inherently adds time. Judging dissolution/blend completion relies on operator experience. Premature addition can cause defects and rework cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of conflict among component-addition instructions available to the operator
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting addition instructions can misdirect charging (relevance), but usually caught before harm; capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Controlled master batch records reduce but do not eliminate conflicting or ambiguous addition instructions. Resolving conflicting instructions mid-batch stalls the operator. Detecting and reconciling instruction conflicts demands experienced judgement. Acting on a wrong instruction can scrap a batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which batch temperature exceeds the upper processing limit
rows.imp_rat: Temperature exceeding the upper limit can degrade actives (severity) and derail dissolution (relevance); bounded by cap.
rows.sat_rat: Jacketed vessels with controlled heating reliably hold temperature below the upper limit. Jacket control responds without operator delay. Little skill needed with automated temperature control loops. Overheating can degrade active and scrap the batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which batch temperature falls below the lower processing limit
rows.imp_rat: Temperature below the lower limit can stall dissolution (relevance) with moderate product impact; held at cap.
rows.sat_rat: Controlled jacket heating keeps temperature above the lower limit consistently. Heating loops correct low temperature promptly. Automated control needs minimal operator judgement. Under-temperature slows dissolution but rarely destroys material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of heat-transfer medium crossing the vessel boundary into the bulk solution
rows.imp_rat: Heat-transfer medium crossing into the bulk is a serious contamination event (severity); relevance moderate; capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Double-wall design, pressure tests and monitoring make HTM ingress very rare. Jacket integrity is verified periodically, not per batch delay. Detecting jacket breach needs some monitoring awareness. HTM crossover contaminates and scraps a batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of difference between indicated and actual batch temperature
rows.imp_rat: Temperature indication error can affect dissolution conditions (relevance) and product (severity), but bounded here by ranking cap.
rows.sat_rat: Routine calibration of RTDs keeps indicated-versus-actual temperature error small and controlled. Instrument checks are periodic and do not delay batches. Reading calibrated instruments requires little skill. Undetected temperature error can degrade or scrap product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of vessel pressure exceeding safe operating limits during compounding
rows.imp_rat: Vessel pressure exceeding safe limits risks rupture and injury (severity); relevance to blending moderate; capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Rated vessels with relief and monitoring make overpressure very rare in atmospheric compounding. Pressure relief acts automatically without delay. No special skill needed with relief devices installed. Overpressure events are rare but costly to equipment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a hazardous reaction caused by incompatible or missequenced component additions
rows.imp_rat: A hazardous reaction from missequenced additions has severe safety consequences (severity); relevance moderate but capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Sequenced batch records and compatibility controls make missequenced hazardous reactions rare. Verifying sequence before addition adds brief checks. Recognizing incompatible additions requires trained chemistry judgement. A hazardous reaction can destroy the batch and endanger staff.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of vessel containment loss exposing personnel to batch materials
rows.imp_rat: Containment loss exposing personnel is a catastrophic safety event (severity) but low relevance to the blending function itself.
rows.sat_rat: Closed vessels and integrity checks make personnel-exposing containment loss rare. Containment is designed-in, adding no runtime time. Little operator skill needed once containment is engineered. Containment loss is a safety and material cost, rarely realized.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of flammable material igniting in or around the vessel during compounding
rows.imp_rat: Flammable ignition is a catastrophic safety event (severity) but low relevance to the routine blending function.
rows.sat_rat: Area classification, bonding and inerting make ignition events very rare. Ignition controls are always in place, no added time. Little operator skill needed once area is classified and controlled. Ignition is a catastrophic safety and asset cost, rarely realized.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an oxygen-deficient atmosphere developing near the compounding vessel
rows.imp_rat: Oxygen-deficient atmosphere is a personnel-safety hazard (severity) but only loosely tied to the blending job (low relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Room ventilation and inert-gas controls make oxygen-deficient atmospheres near vessels rare. Ventilation is always-on, adding no time. No operator skill needed for area oxygen safety once engineered. Oxygen deficiency is a safety, not material, cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of personnel contacting moving agitator components during vessel access
rows.imp_rat: Contact with moving agitator parts is a severe personnel-injury hazard (severity) but low relevance to achieving homogeneity.
rows.sat_rat: Guarding and agitator interlocks make contact with moving parts uncommon. Interlocks stop agitators before access, adding little time. Little skill needed when guards and interlocks are present. Contact injury is a serious but rare safety cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of component or bulk-solution overflow during charging or blending
rows.imp_rat: Overflow during charging causes material loss and safety hazard (severity); relevance moderate; held at cap.
rows.sat_rat: Vessel sizing margins and level checks make overflow uncommon. Level monitoring prevents overflow without added time. Watching level and rate needs operator attention. Overflow is a direct material and cleanup loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of batch material consumed by in-process sampling
rows.imp_rat: Batch material consumed by in-process sampling is a minor yield/waste issue; low relevance and low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Sampling volumes are well-defined in procedures and rarely cause variation in outcome. Sampling itself is quick and does not materially delay the batch. Drawing a representative sample of minimal volume needs modest technique that most operators have. Sample volumes are small relative to batch size, so material loss to in-process sampling is inherently low today.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminants transferring from vessel product-contact surfaces into the batch
rows.imp_rat: Contaminant transfer from contact surfaces threatens product safety (severity); relevance moderate; held at cap.
rows.sat_rat: Validated CIP/cleaning and swab checks make product-contact contamination transfer uncommon. Cleaning verification precedes use without in-batch delay. Cleaning validation removes reliance on operator skill during runs. Surface-borne contamination can scrap a batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminant ingress through vessel seals, ports, or closures during compounding
rows.imp_rat: Ingress through seals/ports contaminates the batch (severity); relevance to blending moderate; capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Sanitary closed vessels with gasketed ports make contaminant ingress uncommon. Closure integrity is designed-in, not a runtime delay. Ensuring ports and closures are sealed needs routine diligence. Ingress contamination can scrap a batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a contaminated active or excipient component is charged into the vessel
rows.imp_rat: A contaminated component charged in spoils the whole batch (severity); relevance to blending moderate; held at cap.
rows.sat_rat: Released, tested materials make contaminated-component charging uncommon though not eliminated. Incoming testing occurs before charging, not delaying the run. Relies on QC testing rather than operator skill. A contaminated component charged scraps the batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that sample withdrawal carries contamination back into the batch
rows.imp_rat: Sample withdrawal carrying contamination back into the batch can spoil finished product (severity); relevance moderate; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Dedicated sampling devices and technique reduce but do not eliminate back-contamination risk. Aseptic withdrawal adds careful handling time. Preventing back-contamination during withdrawal needs aseptic skill. Back-contamination can scrap the batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of microbial proliferation during handling and pre-transfer holding
rows.imp_rat: Microbial proliferation in holding is a real product-safety consequence (severity) but tangential to the mixing act; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Validated hold times, bioburden limits and clean design keep proliferation low but excursions still occur. Enforced hold-time limits constrain how long material sits, occasionally forcing faster processing. Aseptic and hygienic handling demands trained, disciplined operators. Microbial growth can scrap a batch, a major loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of inconsistency among vessel, batch, material, and quality records used for blend-quality risk evaluation
rows.imp_rat: Record inconsistency for risk evaluation is a data-integrity concern peripheral to the physical job.
rows.sat_rat: Fragmented systems and manual transcription commonly produce inconsistent records, and integrated MES coverage is still uneven across sites. Reconciling records across systems is time-consuming but the failure being reduced is inconsistency, not delay. Cross-checking vessel, batch, material and quality records demands attentive, knowledgeable reviewers. Disparate legacy systems impose reconciliation labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in the homogeneity and GMP criteria used to judge blend-quality risk
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in GMP/homogeneity risk criteria is an assessment-framework issue only indirectly tied to the job.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized specifications reduce ambiguity for common cases, but edge cases and qualitative homogeneity criteria still force subjective judgment. Ambiguity resolution consumes review and clarification time but the core issue is not speed. When acceptance criteria are vague, disposition leans heavily on scarce expert interpretation, which is exactly the burden this need targets. Clarifying criteria costs mainly staff effort rather than materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of variation among risk judgments for equivalent vessel, formulation, and batch conditions
rows.imp_rat: Variation in risk judgments is an assessment-consistency issue with limited direct blending impact.
rows.sat_rat: Without strong decision rules, equivalent conditions get judged differently by different reviewers, and consistency remains weakly controlled. Achieving consistent judgments is not primarily about speed. Risk judgments still rely on individual expert interpretation, so variation among assessors is a persistent skill-dependent problem. Standardization efforts cost training time but little material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of homogeneity determinations lacking complete traceable source records
rows.imp_rat: Homogeneity determinations lacking traceable records is a documentation gap, indirect to the physical job.
rows.sat_rat: Homogeneity determinations sometimes lack complete traceable source records where systems are fragmented, a moderate recurring gap. Compiling source records takes time but completeness is the concern. Ensuring complete traceable source records requires knowledgeable documentation practice. Documentation labor cost, standard.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of batch and monitoring records with missing or inconsistent validation evidence
rows.imp_rat: Missing validation evidence in records is a data-integrity gap peripheral to producing the blend.
rows.sat_rat: Missing or inconsistent validation evidence in batch records remains a frequent audit finding, indicating only moderate control. Filling evidence gaps consumes time but the metric targets completeness, not duration. Ensuring validation evidence is complete requires trained reviewers who know what is required. Documentation labor is significant but standard.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that validation-relevant deviations affecting dissolution or blending remain unresolved
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved validation deviations are a compliance-backlog issue peripheral to producing the blend.
rows.sat_rat: CAPA systems track deviations, but backlog and slow closure are chronic industry problems that leave many open too long. Unresolved validation-relevant deviations lingering is an open-duration problem the metric targets directly. Resolving these deviations demands cross-functional validation and quality expertise. Prolonged open deviations tie up resources and delay release, carrying real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that required validation-state assessments for dissolution and blending remain overdue
rows.imp_rat: Overdue validation-state assessments are a compliance-timing issue peripheral to the physical job.
rows.sat_rat: Periodic review programs exist but assessments frequently slip past due dates when resources are stretched. Overdue validation-state assessments are literally a schedule-adherence duration problem. Determining validation state requires qualified validation specialists whose availability is limited. Validation labor is costly but planned.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of candidate capability claims unsupported under specified process conditions
rows.imp_rat: Unsupported capability claims are a qualification-documentation issue, remote from the core blending outcome.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor capability claims are frequently accepted without process-specific verification, leaving unsupported assertions. Validating each capability claim under process conditions extends evaluation time. Judging which vendor claims lack support needs experienced critical review. Unsupported claims lead to costly capability shortfalls.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of product-contact vessel surface absent from candidate cleanability evidence
rows.imp_rat: Contact surface absent from cleanability evidence is a coverage-documentation gap, indirectly tied to blending.

Assembling cleanability evidence for all contact surfaces adds documentation time. Evaluating cleanability coverage uses standard cleaning-validation expertise. Missing evidence forces costly revalidation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of mixing beyond achievement of dissolution and homogeneity criteria

rows.sat_rat: Endpoint-based stopping is reasonably established but conservative padding still adds unnecessary mixing time. Excess mixing beyond endpoint is a clear, measurable time inefficiency. Recognizing the true endpoint to stop needs moderate process judgement. Over-mixing wastes energy and occupies capacity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of specified vessel capabilities lacking traceable sources during qualification review
rows.imp_rat: Untraceable capability sources are a qualification-documentation gap remote from blending.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between qualification acceptance limits and the validated processing envelope for the intended bulk solution
rows.imp_rat: Qualification-limit mismatch is a validation-alignment concern, peripheral to daily blending.
rows.sat_rat: Limits and processing envelopes are frequently developed separately, so mismatches recur and are caught inconsistently. Reconciling acceptance limits with the validated envelope requires iterative alignment that slows sign-off. Matching limits to the processing envelope demands validation SMEs who understand both product and equipment. Mismatch corrections cost review hours rather than materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of qualification criteria lacking traceability to process risks or validated processing requirements
rows.imp_rat: Qualification criteria lacking traceability is a documentation-rigor gap peripheral to daily blending.
rows.sat_rat: Traceability is expected under QRM but often partial, leaving criteria without clear links to validated requirements. Building traceability matrices linking criteria to risks is labor-intensive. Establishing risk-to-criterion traceability requires disciplined quality-risk-management expertise. Traceability work is analyst time with little material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which cleanability evidence differs from intended formulations and operating conditions
rows.imp_rat: Cleanability evidence differing from actual formulations is a validation-representativeness issue, indirect to the job.
rows.sat_rat: Coupon studies and worst-case selection often approximate rather than replicate real operating conditions, so gaps between evidence and reality remain a recurring regulatory vulnerability. Aligning cleanability evidence with actual formulations may delay release when representativeness is challenged in review or audit. Judging whether validation coupons and conditions truly represent production requires sophisticated validation science that is scarce and audit-sensitive. Generating more representative evidence increases study scope and cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of repeat cleaning cycles required to meet hygienic acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Repeat cleaning cycles are an efficiency loss with minimal effect on the blending job itself.
rows.sat_rat: Validated cycles usually pass on the first attempt for established products, but formulation variability and marginal limits still cause enough reruns that this remains only acceptably served. Repeat cleaning cycles directly extend vessel turnaround, and status-quo CIP recipes still trigger reruns when acceptance criteria are marginally missed. Diagnosing why a cycle failed and adjusting parameters relies on experienced cleaning-validation staff rather than automated correction. Each repeat cycle consumes additional water, chemicals, and energy, adding avoidable cost per event.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of manual cleaning interventions required for vessel product-contact surfaces
rows.imp_rat: Manual cleaning interventions are a labor/efficiency concern with minimal blend-quality relevance.
rows.sat_rat: Automated CIP has largely displaced manual cleaning on modern jacketed tanks, but manual touch-up of ports, agitators, and upper heads still recurs and reintroduces operator-dependent variability. Manual cleaning interventions are labor-intensive and slow, extending turnaround whenever CIP cannot fully reach a surface. Manual cleaning depends heavily on operator technique and consistency, making outcomes vulnerable to human variability and demanding trained, attentive personnel. Manual interventions add labor hours and gowning/consumable cost each occurrence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of conflicting conclusions from vessel surface, geometry, and cleanability evidence
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting cleanability-evidence conclusions are an evaluation ambiguity remote from the mixing job.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the cleanability rationale cannot be supported with auditable evidence
rows.imp_rat: Unsupportable cleanability rationale is an evidence-quality gap remote from the blending job.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of conflicting residue-removal conclusions during vessel evaluation
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting residue-removal conclusions are cleaning-evaluation ambiguity, remote from blending.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of undissolved active or excipient particles remaining at the blend endpoint

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in vessel pressure, temperature, agitation, or contents information

rows.sat_rat: Cleaning validation and closed processing keep such discards uncommon. Discarding and re-cleaning after contamination costs time. Preventing vessel-introduced contamination needs disciplined technique. Discarded contaminated batch material is a large direct loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of batch material rendered unusable by incorrect component quantities

rows.sat_rat: Weighing checks make quantity-driven scrap uncommon. Reworking or discarding after quantity errors costs time. Accurate quantities depend on careful weighing. Material rendered unusable by wrong quantities is a direct loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of manual handling events that introduce operator-borne contamination into the batch
rows.imp_rat: Operator-borne contamination from manual handling has real quality stakes (some severity) but is held below the prior batch by the mandated ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Manual handling events remain a leading, only partly controlled contamination source. Careful handling to avoid operator-borne contamination adds time. Aseptic manual handling depends heavily on operator discipline. Operator-borne contamination can scrap a batch.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration the bulk solution remains outside validated compounding limits during the disruption

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of vessel, material, formulation, or control changes and deviations implemented without blend-quality risk evaluation

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of nonconforming batches classified as meeting homogeneity specifications

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of vessel capability specified beyond defined formulation and process requirements

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of unnecessary restrictiveness in specified mixing requirements

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that product or cleaning-agent residues remain above defined acceptance limits at worst-case vessel locations after cleaning

rows.sat_rat: Source verification during review catches many issues, but unverifiable claims still surface in audits. Verifying claims against source records is slow, document-by-document work. Auditing claims to source requires meticulous QA reviewers. Verification is labor-intensive but not material-heavy.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved deviations in records supporting vessel qualification
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved deviations in qualification records are documentation defects, tangential to the mixing job; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation management systems reliably track and close most deviations before recommendation. Resolving record deviations extends qualification closure time. Deviation resolution needs QA judgement that is generally available. Deviation handling is labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of delay in receiving qualification evidence needed for the vessel recommendation
rows.imp_rat: Delay receiving qualification evidence is a timing concern, peripheral to producing homogeneity; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Evidence delivery timing is unpredictable and frequently late, poorly served by current practice. Waiting on vendor and internal qualification evidence is a chronic, hard-to-compress delay. Expediting evidence needs coordination skill but not deep technical expertise. Delays carry schedule and carrying costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of product-contact components omitted from the compatibility assessment
rows.imp_rat: Contact components omitted from compatibility assessment is a documentation-completeness gap, remote from executing the blend and low in direct impact.
rows.sat_rat: BOM-driven compatibility reviews are standard but minor components are still occasionally omitted. Cataloguing all product-contact components takes methodical time. Ensuring completeness needs component-level familiarity with the vessel BOM. Component review is administrative cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of change in product-contact surface compatibility after repeated processing and sanitation exposure
rows.imp_rat: Compatibility change after repeated processing is a long-term material-stability concern, marginal to a single blend and low in immediate consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Repeated-exposure compatibility change is rarely fully characterized, leaving a persistent gap. Assessing compatibility change over repeated exposure requires long-term or accelerated studies. Predicting aging effects needs specialized materials-degradation expertise. Aging studies consume material and extended lab time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved discrepancies in product-contact material identity and composition records
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies in material identity records are documentation ambiguity, tangential to blending; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Material certificates and MTRs usually resolve identity, though discrepancies occasionally remain unresolved. Reconciling material identity records adds documentation-review time. Verifying material identity needs QA and materials familiarity. Mostly administrative cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of compatibility variation across material lots and fabricated surface conditions
rows.imp_rat: Compatibility variation across lots/surfaces is a material-variability concern remote from the current blend and low in direct impact.
rows.sat_rat: Lot and surface-condition variation is seldom explicitly qualified, so this remains poorly served. Testing across material lots and surface conditions adds substantial time. Judging lot-to-lot and finish variation needs deep materials expertise. Multi-lot testing is material- and cost-intensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of qualification acceptance criteria lacking objective pass-or-fail definitions
rows.imp_rat: Acceptance criteria lacking objective pass/fail definitions is a qualification-clarity issue peripheral to blending and low in consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Objective acceptance criteria are the norm in good protocols, yet ambiguous criteria still appear and cause disputes. Writing objective pass/fail criteria upfront takes time but is a one-time protocol effort. Converting vague requirements into objective criteria demands experienced validation authors, a recurring skill dependency. This is protocol-authoring labor with negligible material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cleaning, sterilization, and residue-control requirements lacking explicit acceptance limits
rows.imp_rat: Cleaning/residue requirements lacking acceptance limits is a specification-clarity gap peripheral to the blend and low in direct consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Acceptance limits are standard in cleaning validation, though some requirements still lack explicit limits. Defining explicit acceptance limits for all cleaning/sterilization requirements takes protocol time. Setting residue and sterilization limits needs validation and toxicology input. Limit derivation is analyst labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical vessel measurements lacking requirements for accuracy, calibration status, or record integrity
rows.imp_rat: Measurements lacking accuracy/calibration requirements is a metrology-specification gap, peripheral to the blend and low in immediate consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Calibration and data-integrity requirements are well established, so critical measurements rarely lack them. Documenting accuracy, calibration, and data-integrity requirements adds setup time. Specifying metrology and data-integrity requirements needs calibration expertise. Calibration programs carry ongoing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of unresolved conflict among quality, validation, engineering, production, and user requirements
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved conflict among stakeholder requirements is a requirements-alignment issue, remote from blending; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Cross-functional review forums exist but unresolved requirement conflicts commonly linger into recommendation. Resolving cross-functional requirement conflicts is time-consuming negotiation. Reconciling competing stakeholder requirements demands strong facilitation and technical judgement. Conflict resolution is meeting and labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of qualification-criteria rework caused by requirement changes after vessel evaluation begins
rows.imp_rat: Qualification-criteria rework from requirement changes is a process-rework concern, peripheral to the CFJ; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Change control limits uncontrolled changes but post-start requirement shifts still trigger recurring rework. Late requirement changes force rework that directly extends the evaluation timeline. Managing change impact needs experienced change-control judgement. Rework consumes labor and can waste completed studies.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of mismatch between the evaluated vessel configuration and the configuration available for purchase
rows.imp_rat: Configuration mismatch between evaluated and purchasable vessel is a procurement discrepancy, low relevance to homogeneity; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Change control usually keeps configurations aligned, though vendor spec drift occasionally causes mismatch. Confirming evaluated-versus-purchasable configuration adds verification time. Detecting configuration mismatch needs procurement and technical coordination. Mismatch can cause costly reprocurement.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of qualification delay caused by conflicts with facility constraints or adjacent processing stages
rows.imp_rat: Qualification delay from facility conflicts is a scheduling concern, remote from the mixing job; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Facility-driven qualification delays are common and only partially mitigated by scheduling practice. Facility and adjacent-stage conflicts routinely delay qualification scheduling. Coordinating around facility constraints needs planning skill. Delays incur schedule cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that vessel ranking changes when unverified evaluation assumptions are corrected
rows.imp_rat: Vessel ranking changing when assumptions corrected is an evaluation-robustness concern, remote from mixing; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Unverified assumptions are seldom stress-tested, so ranking instability is a real, under-addressed risk. Testing sensitivity of ranking to assumptions adds analysis time. Assessing how corrected assumptions shift rankings needs analytical rigor rarely applied. Sensitivity analysis is labor cost.

rows.imp_rat: Stakeholders perceiving inconsistency in the influencer's rationale is an influence-credibility error unrelated to the blend task (low relevance); consequences are perception-only and capped (low severity).

rows.imp_rat: Qualification failure attributed to the advisor is a blame concern, remote from producing homogeneity; capped at 1.0.

rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost when vessel causes validation rework is reputational, remote from the CFJ; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which the advisor is bypassed in subsequent vessel decisions after qualification evidence challenges a recommendation
rows.imp_rat: The advisor bypassed after a challenged recommendation is a role-continuity error unrelated to the mixing task (low relevance); consequences are standing-only and capped (low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which quality and validation leadership challenges the advisor's criteria as unfit for qualification or process-validation needs
rows.imp_rat: Quality leadership challenging the advisor's criteria is an advisory-credibility error only tangential to the mixing job (low relevance), with consequences confined to influence loss and capped by ranking (low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of water, cleaning agent, and energy consumed during validated vessel cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Water/cleaning-agent/energy consumption is a resource-efficiency concern, not central to homogeneity; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Consumption is consistent and predictable, but conservative overuse to guarantee cleanliness means efficiency is rarely optimized. Utility consumption is a resource issue rather than a time issue, with negligible schedule impact. Optimizing water and chemical use requires some engineering effort but no scarce expertise once recipes are set. Validated cycles are typically designed for assurance rather than minimal utility use, so water, CIP chemicals, and energy are often consumed conservatively beyond strict need.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of operator labor required for vessel cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Operator labor for cleaning is a labor-efficiency matter, not central to dissolution; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Reduced operator involvement also lowers human-error variability, making automated cleaning both cheaper and more consistent in labor terms. Automated CIP already removes most cleaning labor time from the operator's day for standard cycles. Where labor remains it is manual and technique-dependent, but CIP has reduced reliance on skilled cleaning effort substantially. Automation has cut recurring operator labor cost markedly, leaving mainly residual manual touch-points and oversight.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of vessel unavailability during cleaning and release activities
rows.imp_rat: Vessel unavailability during cleaning/release is throughput timing, peripheral to the CFJ; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Turnaround times are fairly predictable under validated procedures, but analytical release lead times keep total unavailability longer than desired. Cleaning plus sampling, analysis, and release commonly hold vessels out of service for extended periods, the core loss this need targets. Scheduling and release decisions need coordination expertise but are routine for planning staff. Downtime represents significant opportunity cost in capacity, though it is expected and planned for.
